A probabilistic Chinese Remainder Theorem (CRT)-based Solver converts the model's predictions into concrete integers, yielding a 7.4-fold improvement in next-term prediction over the tokenised-Transformer baseline (Top-1: 19.09% vs. 2.59%). Modulo spectrum analysis reveals a strong negative correlation between Normalised Information Gain (NIG) and Euler's totient ratio $\varphi(m)/m$ ($r = -0.851$, $p < 10^{-28}$), providing empirical evidence that composite moduli capture OEIS arithmetic structure more efficiently via CRT aggregation.

AI.tldr: IntSeqBERT: Dual-stream Transformer for OEIS integer sequences using magnitude and modulo embeddings, achieving 95.85% magnitude accuracy and 50.38% modulo accuracy, with 7.4x improvement in next-term prediction over tokenized baselines.
AI.motivation: Standard tokenized models struggle with OEIS sequences due to out-of-vocabulary values and inability to exploit periodic arithmetic structure in sequences spanning from small constants to astronomical values.
AI.method: Dual-stream Transformer encoder with continuous log-scale magnitude embeddings and sin/cos modulo embeddings for 100 residues (moduli 2-101), fused via FiLM. Three prediction heads: magnitude regression, sign classification, and modulo prediction for 100 moduli.
AI.result: 95.85% magnitude accuracy and 50.38% Mean Modulo Accuracy (MMA), outperforming tokenized Transformer by +8.9pt and +4.5pt. CRT-based Solver yields 7.4x improvement in next-term prediction (19.09% vs 2.59%). Strong correlation between information gain and Euler's totient ratio.
AI.conclusion: IntSeqBERT effectively models OEIS sequences by combining magnitude and modular arithmetic representations, with modulo stream providing significant performance gains. Composite moduli capture arithmetic structure more efficiently via CRT aggregation.

title: PRISM: Personalized Refinement of Imitation Skills for Manipulation via Human Instructions
comment: 10 pages, 3 figures, Accepted for publication at European Robotics Forum 2026
summary: This paper presents PRISM: an instruction-conditioned refinement method for imitation policies in robotic manipulation. This approach bridges Imitation Learning (IL) and Reinforcement Learning (RL) frameworks into a seamless pipeline, such that an imitation policy on a broad generic task, generated from a set of user-guided demonstrations, can be refined through reinforcement to generate new unseen fine-grain behaviours. The refinement process follows the Eureka paradigm, where reward functions for RL are iteratively generated from an initial natural-language task description. Presented approach, builds on top of this mechanism to adapt a refined IL policy of a generic task to new goal configurations and the introduction of constraints by adding also human feedback correction on intermediate rollouts, enabling policy reusability and therefore data efficiency. Results for a pick-and-place task in a simulated scenario show that proposed method outperforms policies without human feedback, improving robustness on deployment and reducing computational burden.

AI.tldr: PRISM is an instruction-conditioned refinement method that combines imitation learning and reinforcement learning to adapt generic robotic manipulation policies to new fine-grained behaviors using natural language instructions and human feedback.
AI.motivation: To bridge imitation learning and reinforcement learning for robotic manipulation, enabling reuse of imitation policies for new tasks without extensive retraining, improving data efficiency and robustness.
AI.method: Combines IL and RL in a pipeline: 1) Train imitation policy on generic task from demonstrations, 2) Refine using RL with reward functions generated from natural language descriptions (Eureka paradigm), 3) Add human feedback correction on intermediate rollouts to adapt to new goals/constraints.
AI.result: Outperforms policies without human feedback in simulated pick-and-place tasks, improves robustness on deployment, and reduces computational burden.
AI.conclusion: PRISM enables efficient policy refinement and reuse through the combination of imitation learning, reinforcement learning with language-generated rewards, and human feedback, achieving better performance with less computation.

title: Evolving Medical Imaging Agents via Experience-driven Self-skill Discovery
summary: Clinical image interpretation is inherently multi-step and tool-centric: clinicians iteratively combine visual evidence with patient context, quantify findings, and refine their decisions through a sequence of specialized procedures. While LLM-based agents promise to orchestrate such heterogeneous medical tools, existing systems treat tool sets and invocation strategies as static after deployment. This design is brittle under real-world domain shifts, across tasks, and evolving diagnostic requirements, where predefined tool chains frequently degrade and demand costly manual re-design. We propose MACRO, a self-evolving, experience-augmented medical agent that shifts from static tool composition to experience-driven tool discovery. From verified execution trajectories, the agent autonomously identifies recurring effective multi-step tool sequences, synthesizes them into reusable composite tools, and registers these as new high-level primitives that continuously expand its behavioral repertoire. A lightweight image-feature memory grounds tool selection in a visual-clinical context, while a GRPO-like training loop reinforces reliable invocation of discovered composites, enabling closed-loop self-improvement with minimal supervision. Extensive experiments across diverse medical imaging datasets and tasks demonstrate that autonomous composite tool discovery consistently improves multi-step orchestration accuracy and cross-domain generalization over strong baselines and recent state-of-the-art agentic methods, bridging the gap between brittle static tool use and adaptive, context-aware clinical AI assistance. Code will be available upon acceptance.

AI.tldr: MACRO is a self-evolving medical AI agent that autonomously discovers and synthesizes effective multi-step tool sequences into reusable composite tools, enabling adaptive clinical image interpretation without manual redesign.
AI.motivation: Clinical image interpretation requires iterative, multi-step tool use, but existing LLM-based agents have static tool sets that degrade under real-world domain shifts, tasks, and evolving diagnostic requirements, demanding costly manual re-design.
AI.method: MACRO autonomously identifies effective multi-step tool sequences from verified execution trajectories, synthesizes them into reusable composite tools, and registers these as new high-level primitives. It uses image-feature memory for visual-clinical context and a GRPO-like training loop to reinforce reliable composite tool invocation.
AI.result: Extensive experiments across diverse medical imaging datasets show that autonomous composite tool discovery consistently improves multi-step orchestration accuracy and cross-domain generalization over strong baselines and state-of-the-art agentic methods.
AI.conclusion: MACRO bridges the gap between brittle static tool use and adaptive, context-aware clinical AI assistance by enabling closed-loop self-improvement with minimal supervision through experience-driven tool discovery.

title: DreamCAD: Scaling Multi-modal CAD Generation using Differentiable Parametric Surfaces
summary: Computer-Aided Design (CAD) relies on structured and editable geometric representations, yet existing generative methods are constrained by small annotated datasets with explicit design histories or boundary representation (BRep) labels. Meanwhile, millions of unannotated 3D meshes remain untapped, limiting progress in scalable CAD generation. To address this, we propose DreamCAD, a multi-modal generative framework that directly produces editable BReps from point-level supervision, without CAD-specific annotations. DreamCAD represents each BRep as a set of parametric patches (e.g., Bézier surfaces) and uses a differentiable tessellation method to generate meshes. This enables large-scale training on 3D datasets while reconstructing connected and editable surfaces. Furthermore, we introduce CADCap-1M, the largest CAD captioning dataset to date, with 1M+ descriptions generated using GPT-5 for advancing text-to-CAD research. DreamCAD achieves state-of-the-art performance on ABC and Objaverse benchmarks across text, image, and point modalities, improving geometric fidelity and surpassing 75% user preference. Code and dataset will be publicly available.

AI.tldr: DreamCAD is a multi-modal generative framework that produces editable BReps from point-level supervision without CAD annotations, using parametric patches and differentiable tessellation, achieving SOTA performance on CAD benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing CAD generative methods are limited by small annotated datasets with explicit design histories or BRep labels, while millions of unannotated 3D meshes remain unused, hindering scalable CAD generation.
AI.method: DreamCAD represents BReps as parametric patches (e.g., Bézier surfaces) and uses differentiable tessellation to generate meshes, enabling large-scale training on 3D datasets while reconstructing connected, editable surfaces. Also introduces CADCap-1M dataset with 1M+ GPT-5 generated descriptions.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on ABC and Objaverse benchmarks across text, image, and point modalities, improving geometric fidelity and surpassing 75% user preference.
AI.conclusion: DreamCAD enables scalable CAD generation without CAD-specific annotations, and the release of CADCap-1M dataset advances text-to-CAD research, with code and dataset to be publicly available.

title: A Retrieval-Assisted Framework for Wireless Localization
comment: 13 pages, 11 figures. This work has been submitted to the IEEE for possible publication
summary: Accurate and robust wireless localization is a key enabler for a wide range of mobile computing applications. Fingerprint-based localization using channel state information (CSI) has attracted significant attention due to its high accuracy and compatibility with existing communication infrastructures. However, traditional similarity-based fingerprinting methods suffer from high computational complexity and limited scalability in high-dimensional CSI spaces, while purely learning-based approaches fail to explicitly exploit correlations among reference fingerprints during inference. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a unified retrieval-assisted fingerprinting localization framework that tightly integrates similarity-based and learning-based paradigms. Specifically, channel charting is employed to project high-dimensional CSI into a low-dimensional latent space, enabling efficient and scalable retrieval of locally correlated reference points (RPs). Building upon the retrieved RPs, a graph attention network (GAT) is designed to explicitly model inter-sample correlations between the query CSI and its associated references, allowing adaptive and geometry-aware feature aggregation for accurate position estimation. Extensive experiments conducted on both real-world indoor and ray-tracing simulated outdoor scenarios demonstrate that the proposed method consistently outperforms state-of-the-art similarity-based and learning-based localization approaches.

AI.tldr: A unified retrieval-assisted fingerprinting framework combining similarity-based and learning-based approaches for accurate wireless localization using CSI, leveraging channel charting for dimensionality reduction and graph attention networks for correlation modeling.
AI.motivation: Traditional fingerprint-based localization methods face challenges: similarity-based approaches have high computational complexity and limited scalability in high-dimensional CSI spaces, while purely learning-based methods fail to explicitly exploit correlations among reference fingerprints during inference. There's a need to integrate both paradigms for better performance.
AI.method: Proposes a unified retrieval-assisted fingerprinting framework that integrates similarity-based and learning-based paradigms. Uses channel charting to project high-dimensional CSI into low-dimensional latent space for efficient retrieval of locally correlated reference points. Then employs a graph attention network (GAT) to model inter-sample correlations between query CSI and retrieved references, enabling adaptive geometry-aware feature aggregation for position estimation.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on real-world indoor and ray-tracing simulated outdoor scenarios demonstrate that the proposed method consistently outperforms state-of-the-art similarity-based and learning-based localization approaches.
AI.conclusion: The proposed unified framework successfully addresses limitations of both similarity-based and learning-based fingerprinting methods by combining their strengths through retrieval-assisted architecture, achieving superior localization performance across diverse scenarios.

title: Belief-Adaptive MAP Detection for Molecular ISI Channels with Heteroscedastic Noise
summary: Inter-symbol interference (ISI) with heteroscedastic, or state-dependent, noise is a defining feature of molecular communication via diffusion (MCvD). However, such noise variance dependency across ISI states has not been systematically considered in prior detector designs. This letter introduces two decoding mechanisms, Belief-Adaptive Maximum A Posteriori (BA-MAP) and Soft BA-MAP, that explicitly incorporate state-dependent means and variances of the molecular count channel. The BA-MAP method derives per-symbol adaptive MAP thresholds based on the receiver's current state beliefs, whereas the Soft BA-MAP approach computes mixture log-likelihood ratios by weighting all possible ISI states. Simulation and information-theoretic analyses confirm that the proposed detectors outperform conventional equalization and fixed-threshold methods, achieving up to 100% throughput improvement under realistic MCvD settings.

AI.tldr: Novel BA-MAP and Soft BA-MAP detectors for molecular communication that account for state-dependent noise variance in ISI, outperforming conventional methods with up to 100% throughput improvement.
AI.motivation: Prior detector designs for molecular communication via diffusion (MCvD) have not systematically considered the heteroscedastic (state-dependent) noise variance across inter-symbol interference (ISI) states, which is a defining feature of MCvD systems.
AI.method: Two decoding mechanisms: 1) BA-MAP (Belief-Adaptive Maximum A Posteriori) derives per-symbol adaptive MAP thresholds based on receiver's current state beliefs; 2) Soft BA-MAP computes mixture log-likelihood ratios by weighting all possible ISI states. Both explicitly incorporate state-dependent means and variances of molecular count channel.
AI.result: Simulation and information-theoretic analyses confirm the proposed detectors outperform conventional equalization and fixed-threshold methods, achieving up to 100% throughput improvement under realistic MCvD settings.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly modeling state-dependent noise variance in ISI is crucial for optimal detection in molecular communication systems, and the proposed BA-MAP and Soft BA-MAP detectors provide significant performance gains over existing approaches.

title: Uni-LVC: A Unified Method for Intra- and Inter-Mode Learned Video Compression
summary: Recent advances in learned video compression (LVC) have led to significant performance gains, with codecs such as DCVC-RT surpassing the H.266/VVC low-delay mode in compression efficiency. However, existing LVCs still exhibit key limitations: they often require separate models for intra and inter coding modes, and their performance degrades when temporal references are unreliable. To address this, we introduce Uni-LVC, a unified LVC method that supports both intra and inter coding with low-delay and random-access in a single model. Building on a strong intra-codec, Uni-LVC formulates inter-coding as intra-coding conditioned on temporal information extracted from reference frames. We design an efficient cross-attention adaptation module that integrates temporal cues, enabling seamless support for both unidirectional (low-delay) and bidirectional (random-access) prediction modes. A reliability-aware classifier is proposed to selectively scale the temporal cues, making Uni-LVC behave closer to intra coding when references are unreliable. We further propose a multistage training strategy to facilitate adaptive learning across various coding modes. Extensive experiments demonstrate that Uni-LVC achieves superior rate-distortion performance in intra and inter configurations while maintaining comparable computational efficiency.

AI.tldr: Uni-LVC is a unified learned video compression method that supports both intra and inter coding with low-delay and random-access modes in a single model, overcoming limitations of existing LVCs that require separate models and degrade with unreliable temporal references.
AI.motivation: Existing learned video compression methods have limitations: they require separate models for intra and inter coding modes, and their performance degrades when temporal references are unreliable. There's a need for a unified approach that can handle both coding modes effectively in various scenarios.
AI.method: Uni-LVC builds on a strong intra-codec and formulates inter-coding as intra-coding conditioned on temporal information from reference frames. It uses an efficient cross-attention adaptation module to integrate temporal cues for both unidirectional (low-delay) and bidirectional (random-access) prediction. A reliability-aware classifier selectively scales temporal cues to behave closer to intra coding when references are unreliable, with a multistage training strategy for adaptive learning across coding modes.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show Uni-LVC achieves superior rate-distortion performance in both intra and inter configurations while maintaining comparable computational efficiency.
AI.conclusion: Uni-LVC provides a unified solution for learned video compression that effectively handles both intra and inter coding modes with adaptive temporal reference handling, offering improved performance over existing approaches.

title: Autocorrelation effects in a stochastic-process model for decision making via time series
summary: Decision makers exploiting photonic chaotic dynamics obtained by semiconductor lasers provide an ultrafast approach to solving multi-armed bandit problems by using a temporal optical signal as the driving source for sequential decisions. In such systems, the sampling interval of the chaotic waveform shapes the temporal correlation of the resulting time series, and experiments have reported that decision accuracy depends strongly on this autocorrelation property. However, it remains unclear whether the benefit of autocorrelation can be explained by a minimal mathematical model. Here, we analyze a stochastic-process model of the time-series-based decision making using the tug-of-war principle for solving the two-armed bandit problem, where the threshold and a two-valued Markov signal evolve jointly. Numerical results reveal an environment-dependent structure: negative (positive) autocorrelation is optimal in reward-rich (reward-poor) environments. These findings show that negative autocorrelation of the time series is advantageous when the sum of the winning probabilities is more than $1$, whereas positive autocorrelation is useful when the sum of the winning probabilities is less than $1$. Moreover, the performance is independent of autocorrelation if the sum of the winning probabilities equals $1$, which is mathematically clarified. This study paves the way for improving the decision-making scheme for reinforcement learning applications in wireless communications and robotics.

AI.tldr: Photonic chaotic decision-making systems show autocorrelation-dependent performance in multi-armed bandit problems, with optimal autocorrelation sign depending on reward environment richness.
AI.motivation: To understand whether the observed autocorrelation benefits in photonic chaotic decision-making systems can be explained by a minimal mathematical model, and to clarify the relationship between autocorrelation properties and decision accuracy in different reward environments.
AI.method: Analysis of a stochastic-process model using the tug-of-war principle for solving two-armed bandit problems, where threshold and two-valued Markov signal evolve jointly. Numerical investigation of how autocorrelation affects decision performance.
AI.result: Reveals environment-dependent structure: negative autocorrelation is optimal in reward-rich environments (sum of winning probabilities > 1), positive autocorrelation is optimal in reward-poor environments (sum < 1), and performance is independent of autocorrelation when sum equals 1.
AI.conclusion: The study provides mathematical clarification of autocorrelation effects in decision-making systems, paving the way for improved reinforcement learning applications in wireless communications and robotics by optimizing temporal correlation properties.

title: Task Parameter Extrapolation via Learning Inverse Tasks from Forward Demonstrations
summary: Generalizing skill policies to novel conditions remains a key challenge in robot learning. Imitation learning methods, while data-efficient, are largely confined to the training region and consistently fail on input data outside it, leading to unpredictable policy failures. Alternatively, transfer learning approaches offer methods for trajectory generation robust to both changes in environment or tasks, but they remain data-hungry and lack accuracy in zero-shot generalization. We address these challenges by framing the problem in the context of task inversion learning and proposing a novel joint learning approach to achieve accurate and efficient knowledge transfer. Our method constructs a common representation of the forward and inverse tasks, and leverages auxiliary forward demonstrations from novel configurations to successfully execute the corresponding inverse tasks, without any direct supervision. We show the extrapolation capabilities of our framework via ablation studies and experiments in simulated and real-world environments that require complex manipulation skills with a diverse set of objects and tools, where we outperform diffusion-based alternatives.

AI.tldr: A novel joint learning approach for robot skill generalization that combines forward and inverse task representations, enabling accurate zero-shot transfer to novel conditions without direct supervision.
AI.motivation: Imitation learning methods fail outside training regions, while transfer learning approaches are data-hungry and lack accuracy in zero-shot generalization. There's a need for methods that can efficiently generalize skills to novel conditions.
AI.method: Frames the problem as task inversion learning, constructs a common representation of forward and inverse tasks, and leverages auxiliary forward demonstrations from novel configurations to execute corresponding inverse tasks without direct supervision.
AI.result: Outperforms diffusion-based alternatives in simulated and real-world environments requiring complex manipulation skills with diverse objects and tools, demonstrating strong extrapolation capabilities.
AI.conclusion: The proposed joint learning approach enables accurate and efficient knowledge transfer for robot skill generalization to novel conditions, addressing limitations of both imitation learning and traditional transfer learning methods.

title: The World Won't Stay Still: Programmable Evolution for Agent Benchmarks
summary: LLM-powered agents fulfill user requests by interacting with environments, querying data, and invoking tools in a multi-turn process. Yet, most existing benchmarks assume static environments with fixed schemas and toolsets, neglecting the evolutionary nature of the real world and agents' robustness to environmental changes. In this paper, we study a crucial problem: how to evolve the agent environment in a scalable and controllable way, thereby better evaluating agents' adaptability to real-world dynamics. We propose ProEvolve, a graph-based framework that makes environment evolution programmable. At its core, a typed relational graph provides a unified, explicit representation of the environment: data, tools, and schema. Under this formalism, adding, removing, or modifying capabilities are expressed as graph transformations that coherently propagate updates across tools, schemas, and data access. Building on this, ProEvolve can (1) program the evolutionary dynamics as graph transformations to generate environments automatically, and (2) instantiate task sandboxes via subgraph sampling and programming. We validate ProEvolve by evolving a single environment into 200 environments and 3,000 task sandboxes, and benchmark representative agents accordingly.

AI.tldr: ProEvolve is a graph-based framework for programmatically evolving agent environments to better evaluate LLM agents' adaptability to real-world dynamics.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks assume static environments with fixed schemas and toolsets, neglecting the evolutionary nature of the real world and agents' robustness to environmental changes. There's a need to evaluate agents' adaptability to real-world dynamics through scalable and controllable environment evolution.
AI.method: ProEvolve uses a typed relational graph as a unified, explicit representation of the environment (data, tools, and schema). Environment evolution is expressed as graph transformations that coherently propagate updates across tools, schemas, and data access. The framework can program evolutionary dynamics as graph transformations to generate environments automatically and instantiate task sandboxes via subgraph sampling and programming.
AI.result: The authors validated ProEvolve by evolving a single environment into 200 environments and 3,000 task sandboxes, and benchmarked representative agents accordingly.
AI.conclusion: ProEvolve provides a programmable framework for scalable and controllable environment evolution, enabling better evaluation of LLM agents' adaptability to real-world dynamics through systematic environment transformations.

title: Adversarial Batch Representation Augmentation for Batch Correction in High-Content Cellular Screening
summary: High-Content Screening routinely generates massive volumes of cell painting images for phenotypic profiling. However, technical variations across experimental executions inevitably induce biological batch (bio-batch) effects. These cause covariate shifts and degrade the generalization of deep learning models on unseen data. Existing batch correction methods typically rely on additional prior knowledge (e.g., treatment or cell culture information) or struggle to generalize to unseen bio-batches. In this work, we frame bio-batch mitigation as a Domain Generalization (DG) problem and propose Adversarial Batch Representation Augmentation (ABRA). ABRA explicitly models batch-wise statistical fluctuations by parameterizing feature statistics as structured uncertainties. Through a min-max optimization framework, it actively synthesizes worst-case bio-batch perturbations in the representation space, guided by a strict angular geometric margin to preserve fine-grained class discriminability. To prevent representation collapse during this adversarial exploration, we introduce a synergistic distribution alignment objective. Extensive evaluations on the large-scale RxRx1 and RxRx1-WILDS benchmarks demonstrate that ABRA establishes a new state-of-the-art for siRNA perturbation classification.

AI.tldr: ABRA is a novel domain generalization method that mitigates biological batch effects in cell painting images through adversarial representation augmentation with geometric margin constraints.
AI.motivation: High-content screening generates massive cell painting images, but technical variations create biological batch effects that degrade deep learning model generalization on unseen data. Existing methods either require additional prior knowledge or struggle with unseen batches.
AI.method: ABRA frames batch mitigation as domain generalization, parameterizing feature statistics as structured uncertainties. It uses min-max optimization to synthesize worst-case bio-batch perturbations in representation space with angular geometric margin constraints to preserve class discriminability, plus a synergistic distribution alignment objective to prevent representation collapse.
AI.result: Extensive evaluations on large-scale RxRx1 and RxRx1-WILDS benchmarks show ABRA establishes new state-of-the-art performance for siRNA perturbation classification.
AI.conclusion: ABRA effectively addresses biological batch effects in cell painting images through adversarial representation augmentation, achieving superior generalization without requiring additional prior knowledge.

title: MAD: A Multimodal and Multi-perspective Affective Dataset with Hierarchical Annotations
summary:
This work presents MAD (Multimodal Affection Dataset), a multimodal emotion dataset designed for affective computing and neurophysiological modeling. MAD is built upon synchronous collection of diverse physiological signals (EEG, ECG, EOG, EMG, PPG, and BCG) together with tri-view RGB-D facial videos, enabling the observation of emotional dynamics from neural, physiological, and behavioral perspectives.
  The dataset consists of synchronized recordings from 18 participants and introduces two key contributions. First, it provides temporally aligned multimodal data that jointly capture central neural activity, peripheral physiological responses, and overt facial expressions. Second, it incorporates a three-level emotion annotation framework spanning stimulus elicitation, subjective cognition, and behavioral expression, supporting joint modeling of the full emotion process.
  To validate the dataset, we conduct systematic benchmark experiments covering intra-subject EEG emotion recognition, cross-subject EEG transfer learning, consistency analysis and emotion classification with cardiac-related signals, multimodal physiological fusion, and multi-view facial emotion recognition. The experimental results demonstrate that MAD supports consistent and comparable performance across both unimodal and multimodal settings, establishing it as a reliable benchmark for emotion recognition and cross-modal affective analysis, and as a valuable resource for studying emotion mechanisms across multiple levels.

AI.tldr: MAD is a multimodal emotion dataset with synchronized physiological signals (EEG, ECG, EOG, EMG, PPG, BCG) and tri-view RGB-D facial videos from 18 participants, featuring a three-level emotion annotation framework for comprehensive affective computing research.
AI.motivation: To create a comprehensive multimodal emotion dataset that enables observation of emotional dynamics from neural, physiological, and behavioral perspectives simultaneously, addressing the need for temporally aligned data across multiple modalities for affective computing and neurophysiological modeling.
AI.method: Built dataset through synchronous collection of diverse physiological signals (EEG, ECG, EOG, EMG, PPG, BCG) with tri-view RGB-D facial videos from 18 participants. Implemented a three-level emotion annotation framework covering stimulus elicitation, subjective cognition, and behavioral expression for joint modeling of the full emotion process.
AI.result: Systematic benchmark experiments show consistent and comparable performance across intra-subject EEG emotion recognition, cross-subject EEG transfer learning, cardiac signal classification, multimodal physiological fusion, and multi-view facial emotion recognition, establishing MAD as a reliable benchmark.
AI.conclusion: MAD provides a valuable resource for emotion recognition, cross-modal affective analysis, and studying emotion mechanisms across multiple levels, with synchronized multimodal data supporting comprehensive affective computing research.

title: Quasi-twisted codes and their connection with additive constacyclic codes over finite fields
summary: In this paper, we study quasi-twisted codes and their relationship with additive constacyclic codes through a polynomial-based approach. We first present a polynomial characterization of quasi-twisted codes over finite fields analogous to quasi-cyclic codes and determine Euclidean, Hermitian, and symplectic duals of quasi-twisted codes with index $2$. Additionally, we provide necessary and sufficient conditions for the self-orthogonality of appropriate quasi-twisted codes. Next, we explore a one-to-one correspondence between quasi-twisted codes of length $lm$ with index $l$ over $\mathbb{F}_q$ and additive constacyclic codes of length $m$ over $\mathbb{F}_{q^l}$. We establish relationships between trace inner products in the additive setting and Euclidean, symplectic inner products in the quasi-twisted setting. Using these relations and the correspondence, we determine the dual of additive constacyclic codes with respect to the trace inner products. As a consequence, we conclude that determining the trace Euclidean dual and trace Hermitian dual of an additive constacyclic code is equivalent to determining the Euclidean and symplectic dual of the corresponding quasi-twisted code.

AI.tldr: This paper establishes connections between quasi-twisted codes and additive constacyclic codes using polynomial methods, characterizing duals and self-orthogonality conditions.
AI.motivation: To bridge the gap between quasi-twisted codes and additive constacyclic codes by developing a unified polynomial framework that reveals their structural relationships and enables dual code analysis.
AI.method: Uses polynomial characterization of quasi-twisted codes, establishes one-to-one correspondence between quasi-twisted codes (length lm, index l) and additive constacyclic codes (length m), and analyzes relationships between different inner products (Euclidean, Hermitian, symplectic, trace).
AI.result: Derived polynomial characterization of quasi-twisted codes, determined duals for index 2 codes, established equivalence between trace duals of additive constacyclic codes and Euclidean/symplectic duals of corresponding quasi-twisted codes, and provided self-orthogonality conditions.
AI.conclusion: The paper successfully connects quasi-twisted and additive constacyclic codes through polynomial methods, showing that analyzing trace duals of additive constacyclic codes reduces to analyzing Euclidean/symplectic duals of corresponding quasi-twisted codes, providing a unified framework for code analysis.

title: Architectural Unification for Polarimetric Imaging Across Multiple Degradations
summary: Polarimetric imaging aims to recover polarimetric parameters, including Total Intensity (TI), Degree of Polarization (DoP), and Angle of Polarization (AoP), from captured polarized measurements. In real-world scenarios, these measurements are frequently affected by diverse degradations such as low-light noise, motion blur, and mosaicing artifacts. Due to the nonlinear dependency of DoP and AoP on the measured intensities, accurately retrieving physically consistent polarimetric parameters from degraded observations remains highly challenging. Existing approaches typically adopt task-specific network architectures tailored to individual degradation types, limiting their adaptability across different restoration scenarios. Moreover, many methods rely on multi-stage processing pipelines that suffer from error accumulation, or operate solely in a single domain (either image or Stokes domain), failing to fully exploit the intrinsic physical relationships between them. In this work, we propose a unified architectural framework for polarimetric imaging that is structurally shared across multiple degradation scenarios. Rather than redesigning network structures for each task, our framework maintains a consistent architectural design while being trained separately for different degradations. The model performs single-stage joint image-Stokes processing, avoiding error accumulation and explicitly preserving physical consistency. Extensive experiments show that this unified architectural design, when trained for specific degradation types, consistently achieves state-of-the-art performance across low-light denoising, motion deblurring, and demosaicing tasks, establishing a versatile and physically grounded solution for degraded polarimetric imaging.

AI.tldr: A unified architectural framework for polarimetric imaging that handles multiple degradation types (low-light noise, motion blur, mosaicing) with consistent architecture, performing joint image-Stokes processing to maintain physical consistency.
AI.motivation: Existing polarimetric imaging methods have limitations: they use task-specific architectures for each degradation type, suffer from error accumulation in multi-stage pipelines, and operate in single domains (image or Stokes) without exploiting their physical relationships. This makes accurate retrieval of physically consistent polarimetric parameters from degraded measurements challenging.
AI.method: Proposes a unified architectural framework with consistent design across multiple degradation scenarios. The model performs single-stage joint image-Stokes processing (avoiding error accumulation) and explicitly preserves physical consistency. It maintains the same architecture but is trained separately for different degradations (low-light denoising, motion deblurring, demosaicing).
AI.result: Extensive experiments show state-of-the-art performance across all three tasks (low-light denoising, motion deblurring, demosaicing). The unified design consistently achieves superior results when trained for specific degradation types.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework establishes a versatile and physically grounded solution for degraded polarimetric imaging, overcoming limitations of existing approaches by providing a unified architecture that maintains physical consistency while achieving top performance across multiple restoration scenarios.

title: Towards Efficient and Stable Ocean State Forecasting: A Continuous-Time Koopman Approach
summary: We investigate the Continuous-Time Koopman Autoencoder (CT-KAE) as a lightweight surrogate model for long-horizon ocean state forecasting in a two-layer quasi-geostrophic (QG) system. By projecting nonlinear dynamics into a latent space governed by a linear ordinary differential equation, the model enforces structured and interpretable temporal evolution while enabling temporally resolution-invariant forecasting via a matrix exponential formulation. Across 2083-day rollouts, CT-KAE exhibits bounded error growth and stable large-scale statistics, in contrast to autoregressive Transformer baselines which exhibit gradual error amplification and energy drift over long rollouts. While fine-scale turbulent structures are partially dissipated, bulk energy spectra, enstrophy evolution, and autocorrelation structure remain consistent over long horizons. The model achieves orders-of-magnitude faster inference compared to the numerical solver, suggesting that continuous-time Koopman surrogates offer a promising backbone for efficient and stable hybrid physical-machine learning climate models.

AI.tldr: CT-KAE is a lightweight Koopman autoencoder for ocean forecasting that uses linear latent dynamics for stable long-term predictions with bounded errors, outperforming Transformers and being much faster than numerical solvers.
AI.motivation: Need efficient and stable surrogate models for long-horizon ocean state forecasting that can handle nonlinear dynamics while maintaining interpretability and computational efficiency compared to traditional numerical solvers and autoregressive models.
AI.method: Continuous-Time Koopman Autoencoder (CT-KAE) projects nonlinear ocean dynamics into a latent space governed by a linear ODE, enabling resolution-invariant forecasting via matrix exponential formulation for the two-layer quasi-geostrophic system.
AI.result: CT-KAE shows bounded error growth and stable large-scale statistics over 2083-day rollouts, maintains consistent bulk energy spectra, enstrophy evolution, and autocorrelation structure, while being orders-of-magnitude faster than numerical solvers.
AI.conclusion: Continuous-time Koopman surrogates offer a promising backbone for efficient and stable hybrid physical-machine learning climate models, balancing accuracy, stability, and computational efficiency for long-term ocean forecasting.

title: RACAS: Controlling Diverse Robots With a Single Agentic System
comment: 7 pages in main text + 1 page of appendices + 1 page of references, 5 figures in main text + 1 figure in appendices, 2 tables in main text
summary: Many robotic platforms expose an API through which external software can command their actuators and read their sensors. However, transitioning from these low-level interfaces to high-level autonomous behaviour requires a complicated pipeline, whose components demand distinct areas of expertise. Existing approaches to bridging this gap either require retraining for every new embodiment or have only been validated across structurally similar platforms. We introduce RACAS (Robot-Agnostic Control via Agentic Systems), a cooperative agentic architecture in which three LLM/VLM-based modules (Monitors, a Controller, and a Memory Curator) communicate exclusively through natural language to provide closed-loop robot control. RACAS requires only a natural language description of the robot, a definition of available actions, and a task specification; no source code, model weights, or reward functions need to be modified to move between platforms. We evaluate RACAS on several tasks using a wheeled ground robot, a recently published novel multi-jointed robotic limb, and an underwater vehicle. RACAS consistently solved all assigned tasks across these radically different platforms, demonstrating the potential of agentic AI to substantially reduce the barrier to prototyping robotic solutions.

AI.tldr: RACAS is a robot-agnostic control system using three LLM/VLM modules that communicate via natural language to control diverse robots without platform-specific retraining.
AI.motivation: Existing robot control systems require complicated pipelines and either need retraining for each new robot or only work on structurally similar platforms, creating barriers to prototyping robotic solutions.
AI.method: RACAS uses three cooperative agentic modules (Monitors, Controller, and Memory Curator) based on LLM/VLM that communicate exclusively through natural language. It only requires a natural language description of the robot, available actions definition, and task specification - no code, model weights, or reward function modifications.
AI.result: RACAS consistently solved all assigned tasks across three radically different platforms: a wheeled ground robot, a novel multi-jointed robotic limb, and an underwater vehicle.
AI.conclusion: RACAS demonstrates the potential of agentic AI to substantially reduce the barrier to prototyping robotic solutions by providing robot-agnostic control that works across diverse platforms without platform-specific modifications.

title: DeepFact: Co-Evolving Benchmarks and Agents for Deep Research Factuality
summary: Search-augmented LLM agents can produce deep research reports (DRRs), but verifying claim-level factuality remains challenging. Existing fact-checkers are primarily designed for general-domain, factoid-style atomic claims, and there is no benchmark to test whether such verifiers transfer to DRRs. Yet building such a benchmark is itself difficult. We first show that static expert-labeled benchmarks are brittle in this setting: in a controlled study with PhD-level specialists, unassisted experts achieve only 60.8% accuracy on a hidden micro-gold set of verifiable claims. We propose Evolving Benchmarking via Audit-then-Score (AtS), where benchmark labels and rationales are explicitly revisable: when a verifier disagrees with the current benchmark, it must submit evidence; an auditor adjudicates the dispute; and accepted revisions update the benchmark before models are scored. Across four AtS rounds, expert micro-gold accuracy rises to 90.9%, indicating experts are substantially more reliable as auditors than as one-shot labelers. We instantiate AtS as DeepFact-Bench, a versioned DRR factuality benchmark with auditable rationales, and DeepFact-Eval, a document-level verification agent (with a grouped lite variant) that outperforms existing verifiers on DeepFact-Bench and transfers well to external factuality datasets.

AI.tldr: Researchers propose Evolving Benchmarking via Audit-then-Score (AtS) to create reliable benchmarks for verifying factuality in deep research reports, showing that expert auditors are more reliable than one-shot labelers.
AI.motivation: Existing fact-checkers are designed for general-domain atomic claims and don't transfer well to deep research reports (DRRs). Building reliable benchmarks for DRR factuality verification is difficult because static expert-labeled benchmarks are brittle, with experts achieving only 60.8% accuracy in controlled studies.
AI.method: Proposed Evolving Benchmarking via Audit-then-Score (AtS) where benchmark labels and rationales are explicitly revisable. When a verifier disagrees with current benchmark, it submits evidence; an auditor adjudicates the dispute; accepted revisions update the benchmark before models are scored. Instantiated as DeepFact-Bench (versioned DRR factuality benchmark) and DeepFact-Eval (document-level verification agent).
AI.result: Across four AtS rounds, expert micro-gold accuracy rose from 60.8% to 90.9%, showing experts are substantially more reliable as auditors than as one-shot labelers. DeepFact-Eval outperforms existing verifiers on DeepFact-Bench and transfers well to external factuality datasets.
AI.conclusion: The Audit-then-Score approach creates more reliable benchmarks for DRR factuality verification by leveraging experts as auditors rather than one-shot labelers, with the DeepFact system demonstrating strong performance and transferability.

title: Post Fusion Bird's Eye View Feature Stabilization for Robust Multimodal 3D Detection
summary:
Camera-LiDAR fusion is widely used in autonomous driving to enable accurate 3D object detection. However, bird's-eye view (BEV) fusion detectors can degrade significantly under domain shift and sensor failures, limiting reliability in real-world deployment. Existing robustness approaches often require modifying the fusion architecture or retraining specialized models, making them difficult to integrate into already deployed systems.
  We propose a Post Fusion Stabilizer (PFS), a lightweight module that operates on intermediate BEV representations of existing detectors and produces a refined feature map for the original detection head. The design stabilizes feature statistics under domain shift, suppresses spatial regions affected by sensor degradation, and adaptively restores weakened cues through residual correction. Designed as a near-identity transformation, PFS preserves performance while improving robustness under diverse camera and LiDAR corruptions. Evaluations on the nuScenes benchmark demonstrate that PFS achieves state-of-the-art results in several failure modes, notably improving camera dropout robustness by +1.2% and low-light performance by +4.4% mAP while maintaining a lightweight footprint of only 3.3 M parameters.

AI.tldr: PFS is a lightweight post-fusion stabilizer module that improves robustness of BEV fusion detectors against domain shift and sensor failures without architectural changes.
AI.motivation: BEV fusion detectors degrade under domain shift and sensor failures, limiting real-world reliability. Existing robustness approaches require architectural modifications or specialized retraining, making integration into deployed systems difficult.
AI.method: PFS operates on intermediate BEV representations of existing detectors, producing refined feature maps for original detection heads. It stabilizes feature statistics under domain shift, suppresses spatial regions affected by sensor degradation, and adaptively restores weakened cues through residual correction as a near-identity transformation.
AI.result: On nuScenes benchmark, PFS achieves SOTA results in several failure modes: +1.2% mAP improvement for camera dropout robustness, +4.4% mAP improvement for low-light performance, while maintaining only 3.3M parameters.
AI.conclusion: PFS provides a lightweight, plug-and-play solution to enhance robustness of existing BEV fusion detectors against domain shifts and sensor failures without requiring architectural modifications or retraining.

title: Set-Prediction-Based J-Peak Detection for Pillow-Based Ballistocardiography
summary:
J-peak detection in ballistocardiography (BCG) is a key component of unobtrusive heart rate monitoring during sleep.
  Most existing approaches formulate this task as a dense time-point segmentation problem and rely on heuristic post-processing to convert continuous responses into discrete peak events, resulting in redundant model structures and sensitivity to parameter settings.
  In this work, we construct and publicly release a pillow-based BCG--ECG dataset consisting of multi-subject, multi-night natural sleep recordings with manually annotated BCG J-peaks.
  Based on this dataset, we propose a set-prediction-based J-peak detection framework that directly models peaks as discrete temporal events, eliminating the need for high-resolution segmentation heads and explicit peak suppression.
  Experimental results show that, under a shared convolutional backbone, the proposed method achieves superior detection performance compared to a U-Net-based segmentation baseline, while substantially reducing model parameters and computational complexity.
  These results indicate that event-level set prediction provides a concise and efficient modeling paradigm for BCG J-peak detection in sleep monitoring.

AI.tldr: A set-prediction-based framework for BCG J-peak detection that directly models peaks as discrete events, outperforming segmentation approaches with fewer parameters.
AI.motivation: Existing BCG J-peak detection methods use dense segmentation with heuristic post-processing, leading to redundant models and sensitivity to parameters. There's a need for more efficient and direct peak detection approaches.
AI.method: Proposed a set-prediction-based J-peak detection framework that directly models peaks as discrete temporal events, eliminating segmentation heads and explicit peak suppression. Created and released a pillow-based BCG-ECG dataset with multi-subject, multi-night sleep recordings and manual annotations.
AI.result: The proposed method achieves superior detection performance compared to U-Net-based segmentation baseline while substantially reducing model parameters and computational complexity.
AI.conclusion: Event-level set prediction provides a concise and efficient modeling paradigm for BCG J-peak detection in sleep monitoring, offering better performance with reduced complexity.

title: The DNA Coverage Depth Problem: Duality, Weight Distributions, and Applications
comment: arXiv admin note: substantial text overlap with arXiv:2507.20639
summary: The coverage depth problem in DNA data storage is about computing the expected number of reads needed to recover all encoded strands. Given a generator matrix of a linear code, this quantity equals the expected number of randomly drawn columns required to obtain full rank. While MDS codes are optimal when they exist, i.e., over large fields, practical scenarios may rely on structured code families defined over small fields. In this work, we develop combinatorial tools to solve the DNA coverage depth problem for various linear codes, based on duality arguments and the notion of extended weight enumerator. Using these methods, we derive closed formulas for the simplex, Hamming, ternary Golay, extended ternary Golay, and first-order Reed-Muller codes. The centerpiece of this paper is a general expression for the coverage depth of a linear code in terms of the weight distributions of its higher-field extensions.

AI.tldr: The paper develops combinatorial methods to compute DNA coverage depth for linear codes using duality and extended weight enumerators, deriving formulas for specific code families.
AI.motivation: DNA data storage requires computing expected reads needed to recover all encoded strands, which equals expected random column draws for full rank. While MDS codes are optimal over large fields, practical DNA storage often uses structured codes over small fields, necessitating analysis tools for these specific code families.
AI.method: Develops combinatorial tools based on duality arguments and extended weight enumerators to solve DNA coverage depth problem for linear codes. Uses these methods to derive closed formulas for specific code families.
AI.result: Derives closed formulas for coverage depth of simplex, Hamming, ternary Golay, extended ternary Golay, and first-order Reed-Muller codes. Presents general expression for coverage depth of any linear code in terms of weight distributions of its higher-field extensions.
AI.conclusion: Provides systematic combinatorial approach to DNA coverage depth problem for linear codes over small fields, with practical formulas for important code families and general theoretical framework connecting coverage depth to extended weight enumerators.

title: Enhancing Neural Video Compression of Static Scenes with Positive-Incentive Noise
summary: Static scene videos, such as surveillance feeds and videotelephony streams, constitute a dominant share of storage consumption and network traffic. However, both traditional standardized codecs and neural video compression (NVC) methods struggle to encode these videos efficiently due to inadequate usage of temporal redundancy and severe distribution gaps between training and test data, respectively. While recent generative compression methods improve perceptual quality, they introduce hallucinated details that are unacceptable in authenticity-critical applications. To overcome these limitations, we propose to incorporate positive-incentive noise into NVC for static scene videos, where short-term temporal changes are reinterpreted as positive-incentive noise to facilitate model finetuning. By disentangling transient variations from the persistent background, structured prior information is internalized in the compression model. During inference, the invariant component requires minimal signaling, thus reducing data transmission while maintaining pixel-level fidelity. Preliminary experiments demonstrate a 73% Bjøntegaard delta (BD) rate saving compared to general NVC models. Our method provides an effective solution to trade computation for bandwidth, enabling robust video transmission under adverse network conditions and economic long-term retention of surveillance footage.

AI.tldr: Proposes adding positive-incentive noise to neural video compression for static scenes, achieving 73% BD-rate savings while maintaining pixel-level fidelity.
AI.motivation: Static scene videos (surveillance, videotelephony) consume massive storage/bandwidth but current codecs struggle with temporal redundancy. Neural video compression suffers from training-test distribution gaps, while generative methods introduce unacceptable hallucinations for authenticity-critical applications.
AI.method: Incorporate positive-incentive noise into NVC for static scenes, where short-term temporal changes are reinterpreted as positive-incentive noise to facilitate model finetuning. Disentangle transient variations from persistent background, internalizing structured prior information in the compression model.
AI.result: Preliminary experiments show 73% Bjøntegaard delta (BD) rate saving compared to general NVC models. The method reduces data transmission while maintaining pixel-level fidelity.
AI.conclusion: Provides effective solution to trade computation for bandwidth, enabling robust video transmission under adverse network conditions and economic long-term retention of surveillance footage.

title: When AI Levels the Playing Field: Skill Homogenization, Asset Concentration, and Two Regimes of Inequality
summary: Generative AI compresses within-task skill differences while shifting economic value toward concentrated complementary assets, creating an apparent paradox: the technology that equalizes individual performance may widen aggregate inequality. We formalize this tension in a task-based model with endogenous education, employer screening, and heterogeneous firms. The model yields two regimes whose boundary depends on AI's technology structure (proprietary vs.\ commodity) and labor market institutions (rent-sharing elasticity, asset concentration). A scenario analysis via Method of Simulated Moments, matching six empirical targets, disciplines the model's quantitative magnitudes; a sensitivity decomposition reveals that the five non-$Δ$Gini moments identify mechanism rates but not the aggregate sign, which at the calibrated parameters is pinned by $m_6$ and $ξ$, while AI's technology structure ($η_1$ vs.\ $η_0$) independently crosses the boundary. The contribution is the mechanism -- not a verdict on the sign. Occupation-level regressions using BLS OEWS data (2019--2023) illustrate why such data cannot test the model's task-level predictions. The predictions are testable with within-occupation, within-task panel data that do not yet exist at scale.

AI.tldr: AI equalizes individual skill differences but may increase aggregate inequality by shifting value to complementary assets, with effects depending on technology structure and labor markets.
AI.motivation: To resolve the paradox where AI reduces individual skill differences yet potentially increases overall inequality, by examining how AI shifts economic value toward concentrated complementary assets.
AI.method: Develop a task-based model with endogenous education, employer screening, and heterogeneous firms; use Method of Simulated Moments with six empirical targets for calibration; conduct sensitivity analysis; test with occupation-level regressions using BLS OEWS data.
AI.result: Model yields two regimes depending on AI's technology structure (proprietary vs. commodity) and labor market institutions; calibration shows aggregate inequality sign depends on specific parameters, with AI's technology structure independently crossing the boundary between regimes.
AI.conclusion: The main contribution is identifying the mechanism (not determining the sign of inequality effect); current occupation-level data cannot test task-level predictions, requiring within-occupation, within-task panel data that doesn't yet exist at scale.

title: Control Lyapunov Functions for Underactuated Soft Robots
comment: 8 pages, 5 figures, 2 tables. Submitted for publication to a conference
summary: Soft and soft-rigid hybrid robots are inherently underactuated and operate under tight actuator limits, making task-space control with stability guarantees challenging. Common nonlinear strategies for soft robots (e.g., those based on PD control) often rely on the assumption of full actuation with no actuator limits. This paper presents a general control framework for task-space regulation and tracking of underactuated soft robots under bounded inputs. The method enforces a rapidly exponentially stabilizing control Lyapunov function as a convex inequality constraint while simultaneously satisfying underactuated full-body dynamics and actuator bounds. We validate the approach in simulation on several platforms spanning increasing underactuation: a simple two link tendon-driven "finger", a trimmed helicoid manipulator, and a highly underactuated spiral robot. We compare against a number of baseline methods from the literature. Results show improved task-space accuracy and consistent Lyapunov convergence under input limits, achieving superior set-point and trajectory-tracking performance.

AI.tldr: A control framework for underactuated soft robots with bounded inputs that enforces rapid exponential stabilization via control Lyapunov functions while satisfying dynamics and actuator constraints.
AI.motivation: Soft and hybrid soft-rigid robots are underactuated with tight actuator limits, making stable task-space control challenging. Existing nonlinear control methods often assume full actuation without limits, which doesn't match real-world constraints.
AI.method: Proposes a control framework that enforces a rapidly exponentially stabilizing control Lyapunov function as a convex inequality constraint while simultaneously satisfying underactuated full-body dynamics and actuator bounds.
AI.result: Validated in simulation on three platforms with increasing underactuation: tendon-driven finger, trimmed helicoid manipulator, and highly underactuated spiral robot. Shows improved task-space accuracy and consistent Lyapunov convergence under input limits compared to baseline methods.
AI.conclusion: The framework achieves superior set-point and trajectory-tracking performance for underactuated soft robots with bounded inputs, addressing the gap between theoretical control methods and practical actuator limitations.

title: An Interactive Multi-Agent System for Evaluation of New Product Concepts
comment: 46 pages, 3 figures + This paper proposes an LLM-based multi-agent system (MAS) for automated evaluation of new product concepts, incorporating retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) and cross-functional virtual agents to assess technical and market feasibility
summary: Product concept evaluation is a critical stage that determines strategic resource allocation and project success in enterprises. However, traditional expert-led approaches face limitations such as subjective bias and high time and cost requirements. To support this process, this study proposes an automated approach utilizing a large language model (LLM)-based multi-agent system (MAS). Through a systematic analysis of previous research on product development and team collaboration, this study established two primary evaluation dimensions, namely technical feasibility and market feasibility. The proposed system consists of a team of eight virtual agents representing specialized domains such as R&D and marketing. These agents use retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) and real-time search tools to gather objective evidence and validate concepts through structured deliberations based on the established criteria. The agents were further fine-tuned using professional product review data to enhance their judgment accuracy. A case study involving professional display monitor concepts demonstrated that the system's evaluation rankings were consistent with those of senior industry experts. These results confirm the usability of the proposed multi-agent-based evaluation approach for supporting product development decisions.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes an LLM-based multi-agent system for automated product concept evaluation, using 8 virtual domain experts to assess technical and market feasibility through RAG and real-time search, achieving expert-level consistency.
AI.motivation: Traditional expert-led product concept evaluation suffers from subjective bias, high time/cost requirements, and limited scalability, creating a need for more objective, efficient automated approaches.
AI.method: Developed an LLM-based multi-agent system with 8 virtual agents representing specialized domains (R&D, marketing, etc.), using retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) and real-time search tools for evidence gathering, with agents fine-tuned on professional product review data.
AI.result: System evaluation rankings for professional display monitor concepts showed consistency with senior industry experts, demonstrating the approach's practical usability for product development decisions.
AI.conclusion: The proposed multi-agent-based evaluation approach provides an effective automated solution for product concept evaluation that can support strategic resource allocation and decision-making in enterprises.

title: Rethinking Concept Bottleneck Models: From Pitfalls to Solutions
summary: Concept Bottleneck Models (CBMs) ground predictions in human-understandable concepts but face fundamental limitations: the absence of a metric to pre-evaluate concept relevance, the "linearity problem" causing recent CBMs to bypass the concept bottleneck entirely, an accuracy gap compared to opaque models, and finally the lack of systematic study on the impact of different visual backbones and VLMs. We introduce CBM-Suite, a methodological framework to systematically addresses these challenges. First, we propose an entropy-based metric to quantify the intrinsic suitability of a concept set for a given dataset. Second, we resolve the linearity problem by inserting a non-linear layer between concept activations and the classifier, which ensures that model accuracy faithfully reflects concept relevance. Third, we narrow the accuracy gap by leveraging a distillation loss guided by a linear teacher probe. Finally, we provide comprehensive analyses on how different vision encoders, vision-language models, and concept sets interact to influence accuracy and interpretability in CBMs. Extensive evaluations show that CBM-Suite yields more accurate models and provides insights for improving concept-based interpretability.

AI.tldr: CBM-Suite addresses four key limitations of Concept Bottleneck Models: no metric for concept relevance evaluation, the linearity problem causing bypass of concept bottleneck, accuracy gap compared to opaque models, and lack of systematic study on visual backbones/VLMs.
AI.motivation: Current Concept Bottleneck Models (CBMs) have fundamental limitations: 1) No metric to pre-evaluate concept relevance before training, 2) "Linearity problem" where recent CBMs bypass the concept bottleneck entirely, 3) Accuracy gap compared to opaque models, and 4) Lack of systematic study on how different visual backbones and vision-language models impact CBMs.
AI.method: CBM-Suite introduces four methodological solutions: 1) Entropy-based metric to quantify concept set suitability, 2) Non-linear layer between concept activations and classifier to resolve linearity problem, 3) Distillation loss guided by linear teacher probe to narrow accuracy gap, and 4) Comprehensive analyses of vision encoders, VLMs, and concept sets interactions.
AI.result: Extensive evaluations show CBM-Suite yields more accurate models and provides insights for improving concept-based interpretability. The framework addresses the fundamental limitations of CBMs while maintaining interpretability.
AI.conclusion: CBM-Suite provides a systematic framework to overcome key challenges in Concept Bottleneck Models, enabling more accurate and interpretable models while offering methodological insights for concept-based interpretability research.

title: U6G XL-MIMO Radiomap Prediction: Multi-Config Dataset and Beam Map Approach
comment: This work has been submitted to the IEEE for possible publication
summary: The upper 6 GHz (U6G) band with XL-MIMO is a key enabler for sixth-generation wireless systems, yet intelligent radiomap prediction for such systems remains challenging. Existing datasets support only small-scale arrays (up to 8x8) with predominantly isotropic antennas, far from the 1024-element directional arrays envisioned for 6G. Moreover, current methods encode array configurations as scalar parameters, forcing neural networks to extrapolate array-specific radiation patterns, which fails when predicting radiomaps for configurations absent from training data. To jointly address data scarcity and generalization limitations, this paper advances XL-MIMO radiomap prediction from three aspects. To overcome data limitations, we construct the first XL-MIMO radiomap dataset containing 78400 radiomaps across 800 urban scenes, five frequency bands (1.8-6.7 GHz), and nine array configurations up to 32x32 uniform planar arrays with directional elements. To enable systematic evaluation, we establish a comprehensive benchmark framework covering practical scenarios from coverage estimation without field measurements to generalization across unseen configurations and environments. To enable generalization to arbitrary beam configurations without retraining, we propose the beam map, a physics-informed spatial feature that analytically computes array-specific coverage patterns. By decoupling deterministic array radiation from data learned multipath propagation, beam maps shift generalization from neural network extrapolation to physics-based computation. Integrating beam maps into existing architectures reduces mean absolute error by up to 60.0% when generalizing to unseen configurations and up to 50.5% when transferring to unseen environments. The complete dataset and code are publicly available at https://lxj321.github.io/MulticonfigRadiomapDataset/.

AI.tldr: First XL-MIMO radiomap dataset with 78,400 radiomaps across 800 urban scenes, 5 frequency bands, and 9 array configurations up to 32x32 directional arrays, plus a physics-informed beam map method that improves generalization by 60% for unseen configurations.
AI.motivation: Existing datasets only support small-scale arrays (up to 8x8) with isotropic antennas, while 6G requires 1024-element directional arrays. Current methods encode array configurations as scalar parameters, forcing neural networks to extrapolate radiation patterns, which fails for configurations absent from training data.
AI.method: 1) Constructed first XL-MIMO radiomap dataset with 78,400 radiomaps across 800 urban scenes, 5 frequency bands (1.8-6.7 GHz), and 9 array configurations up to 32x32 uniform planar arrays with directional elements. 2) Established comprehensive benchmark framework for practical scenarios. 3) Proposed beam map - a physics-informed spatial feature that analytically computes array-specific coverage patterns, decoupling deterministic array radiation from learned multipath propagation.
AI.result: Beam map integration reduces mean absolute error by up to 60.0% when generalizing to unseen configurations and up to 50.5% when transferring to unseen environments. Complete dataset and code are publicly available.
AI.conclusion: The paper addresses both data scarcity and generalization limitations for XL-MIMO radiomap prediction by providing the first large-scale dataset and introducing physics-informed beam maps that enable generalization to arbitrary beam configurations without retraining, shifting from neural network extrapolation to physics-based computation.

title: Asymmetric Stream Allocation and Linear Decodability in MIMO Coded Caching
summary: Coded caching (CC) can transform cache memory at network devices into an active communication resource. Prior studies have shown that CC can significantly enhance the achievable Degrees of Freedom (DoF) in multi-input multi-output (MIMO) systems. To fully exploit MIMO-CC gains across all SNR regimes and enable practical linear receivers, flexible scheduling is required. Existing DoF analysis, scheduling, and linear receiver design, however, largely assume symmetric stream allocations across users. This paper extends the authors' recent work on DoF and linear decodability analysis for MIMO-CC systems by deriving a simple criterion, based on per-user stream allocation, that guarantees linear decodability for both symmetric and non-symmetric bit-level CC schemes. Building on this, we propose a heuristic MIMO-CC delivery and scheduling framework that enables asymmetric stream allocation while adhering to linear decodability, thereby expanding the feasibility region of achievable DoF compared to symmetric-constrained designs.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a flexible scheduling framework for MIMO coded caching systems that enables asymmetric stream allocation while maintaining linear decodability, expanding the achievable DoF region beyond symmetric-constrained designs.
AI.motivation: Existing MIMO coded caching systems largely assume symmetric stream allocations across users, which limits practical implementation with linear receivers and doesn't fully exploit gains across all SNR regimes. Flexible scheduling is needed to enable practical linear receivers while maximizing DoF benefits.
AI.method: The authors extend their prior work by deriving a simple criterion based on per-user stream allocation that guarantees linear decodability for both symmetric and non-symmetric bit-level coded caching schemes. They then propose a heuristic MIMO-CC delivery and scheduling framework that enables asymmetric stream allocation while adhering to this linear decodability criterion.
AI.result: The proposed framework expands the feasibility region of achievable Degrees of Freedom compared to symmetric-constrained designs, enabling more flexible and practical MIMO coded caching implementations.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides a practical solution for implementing MIMO coded caching with linear receivers by enabling asymmetric stream allocation through a flexible scheduling framework that maintains linear decodability, overcoming limitations of symmetric-constrained designs.

title: AI End-to-End Radiation Treatment Planning Under One Second
summary: Artificial intelligence-based radiation therapy (RT) planning has the potential to reduce planning time and inter-planner variability, improving efficiency and consistency in clinical workflows. Most existing automated approaches rely on multiple dose evaluations and corrections, resulting in plan generation times of several minutes. We introduce AIRT (Artificial Intelligence-based Radiotherapy), an end-to-end deep-learning framework that directly infers deliverable treatment plans from CT images and structure contours. AIRT generates single-arc VMAT prostate plans, from imaging and anatomical inputs to leaf sequencing, in under one second on a single Nvidia A100 GPU. The framework includes a differentiable dose feedback, an adversarial fluence map shaping, and a plan generation augmentation to improve plan quality and robustness. The model was trained on more than 10,000 intact prostate cases. Non-inferiority to RapidPlan Eclipse was demonstrated across target coverage and OAR sparing metrics. Target homogeneity (HI = 0.10 $\pm$ 0.01) and OAR sparing were similar to reference plans when evaluated using AcurosXB. These results represent a significant step toward ultra-fast standardized RT planning and a streamlined clinical workflow.

AI.tldr: AIRT is an end-to-end deep learning framework that generates deliverable prostate VMAT treatment plans from CT images in under 1 second, achieving non-inferior quality compared to traditional planning methods.
AI.motivation: To reduce planning time and inter-planner variability in radiation therapy workflows, addressing the limitations of existing automated approaches that take several minutes and require multiple dose evaluations/corrections.
AI.method: End-to-end deep learning framework with differentiable dose feedback, adversarial fluence map shaping, and plan generation augmentation. Trained on 10,000+ intact prostate cases, generates single-arc VMAT plans directly from CT images and structure contours.
AI.result: Generates plans in under 1 second on Nvidia A100 GPU. Demonstrates non-inferiority to RapidPlan Eclipse in target coverage and OAR sparing metrics. Target homogeneity (HI = 0.10 ± 0.01) and OAR sparing similar to reference plans when evaluated with AcurosXB.
AI.conclusion: AIRT represents a significant advancement toward ultra-fast standardized RT planning and streamlined clinical workflows, potentially revolutionizing radiation therapy planning efficiency.

title: Aligning the True Semantics: Constrained Decoupling and Distribution Sampling for Cross-Modal Alignment
summary: Cross-modal alignment is a crucial task in multimodal learning aimed at achieving semantic consistency between vision and language. This requires that image-text pairs exhibit similar semantics. Traditional algorithms pursue embedding consistency to achieve semantic consistency, ignoring the non-semantic information present in the embedding. An intuitive approach is to decouple the embeddings into semantic and modality components, aligning only the semantic component. However, this introduces two main challenges: (1) There is no established standard for distinguishing semantic and modal information. (2) The modality gap can cause semantic alignment deviation or information loss. To align the true semantics, we propose a novel cross-modal alignment algorithm via \textbf{C}onstrained \textbf{D}ecoupling and \textbf{D}istribution \textbf{S}ampling (CDDS). Specifically, (1) A dual-path UNet is introduced to adaptively decouple the embeddings, applying multiple constraints to ensure effective separation. (2) A distribution sampling method is proposed to bridge the modality gap, ensuring the rationality of the alignment process. Extensive experiments on various benchmarks and model backbones demonstrate the superiority of CDDS, outperforming state-of-the-art methods by 6.6\% to 14.2\%.

AI.tldr: CDDS is a novel cross-modal alignment method that uses constrained decoupling and distribution sampling to achieve better semantic consistency between vision and language by separating semantic from non-semantic information.
AI.motivation: Traditional cross-modal alignment methods focus on embedding consistency but ignore non-semantic information in embeddings. Simply decoupling embeddings into semantic and modality components introduces challenges: no clear standard for distinguishing semantic vs modal information, and modality gaps can cause semantic alignment deviation or information loss.
AI.method: CDDS (Constrained Decoupling and Distribution Sampling) uses: (1) A dual-path UNet to adaptively decouple embeddings with multiple constraints for effective separation, and (2) A distribution sampling method to bridge the modality gap and ensure alignment rationality.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on various benchmarks and model backbones show CDDS outperforms state-of-the-art methods by 6.6% to 14.2%.
AI.conclusion: CDDS effectively addresses the limitations of traditional cross-modal alignment by properly separating semantic from non-semantic information and bridging modality gaps, achieving superior semantic consistency between vision and language.

title: RFM-HRI : A Multimodal Dataset of Medical Robot Failure, User Reaction and Recovery Preferences for Item Retrieval Tasks
summary:
While robots deployed in real-world environments inevitably experience interaction failures, understanding how users respond through verbal and non-verbal behaviors remains under-explored in human-robot interaction (HRI). This gap is particularly significant in healthcare-inspired settings, where interaction failures can directly affect task performance and user trust.
