AI.tldr: CylinderSplat introduces a feed-forward framework for panoramic 3D Gaussian Splatting using a cylindrical Triplane representation to better handle 360° scenes and sparse-view scenarios.
AI.motivation: Existing 3DGS methods struggle with panoramic imagery due to multi-view cost volumes that fail with occlusions in sparse views, and standard Cartesian Triplanes cause distortion and aliasing in 360° scenes.
AI.method: Proposes CylinderSplat with a cylindrical Triplane representation aligned with panoramic geometry and Manhattan-world structures. Uses dual-branch architecture: pixel-based branch for well-observed regions and volume-based branch with cylindrical Triplane for occluded/sparse areas. Handles variable input views from single to multiple panoramas.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art results in both single-view and multi-view panoramic novel view synthesis, outperforming previous methods in reconstruction quality and geometric accuracy.
AI.conclusion: CylinderSplat effectively addresses limitations of existing 3DGS methods for panoramic scenes through cylindrical representation and dual-branch architecture, enabling high-quality novel view synthesis for 360° imagery.

title: Reference-guided Policy Optimization for Molecular Optimization via LLM Reasoning
summary: Large language models (LLMs) benefit substantially from supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) in reasoning tasks. However, these recipes perform poorly in instruction-based molecular optimization, where each data point typically provides only a single optimized reference molecule and no step-by-step optimization trajectory. We reveal that answer-only SFT on the reference molecules collapses reasoning, and RLVR provides sparse feedback under similarity constraints due to the model's lack of effective exploration, which slows learning and limits optimization. To encourage the exploration of new molecules while balancing the exploitation of the reference molecules, we introduce Reference-guided Policy Optimization (RePO), an optimization approach that learns from reference molecules without requiring trajectory data. At each update, RePO samples candidate molecules with their intermediate reasoning trajectories from the model and trains the model using verifiable rewards that measure property satisfaction under similarity constraints in an RL manner. Meanwhile, it applies reference guidance by keeping the policy's intermediate reasoning trajectory as context and training only the answer in a supervised manner. Together, the RL term promotes exploration, while the guidance term mitigates reward sparsity and stabilizes training by grounding outputs to references when many valid molecular edits exist. Across molecular optimization benchmarks, RePO consistently outperforms SFT and RLVR baselines (e.g., GRPO), achieving improvements on the optimization metric (Success Rate $\times$ Similarity), improving balance across competing objectives, and generalizing better to unseen instruction styles. Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/tmlr-group/RePO.

AI.tldr: RePO is a new method for molecular optimization that combines reinforcement learning with reference guidance to overcome limitations of SFT and RLVR when only single optimized molecules are available without step-by-step trajectories.
AI.motivation: Standard SFT and RLVR methods perform poorly in instruction-based molecular optimization where only single optimized reference molecules are available without step-by-step trajectories. SFT collapses reasoning and RLVR provides sparse feedback due to lack of effective exploration.
AI.method: Reference-guided Policy Optimization (RePO) combines RL with reference guidance: samples candidate molecules with reasoning trajectories, uses verifiable rewards for property satisfaction under similarity constraints (RL term), and applies reference guidance by keeping policy's intermediate reasoning as context while training only the answer in supervised manner.
AI.result: RePO consistently outperforms SFT and RLVR baselines (including GRPO) across molecular optimization benchmarks, achieving improvements on Success Rate × Similarity metric, better balance across competing objectives, and better generalization to unseen instruction styles.
AI.conclusion: RePO effectively addresses the exploration-exploitation trade-off in molecular optimization by combining RL exploration with reference guidance, overcoming limitations of existing methods when only single optimized molecules are available without trajectories.

title: HarvestFlex: Strawberry Harvesting via Vision-Language-Action Policy Adaptation in the Wild
summary: This work presents the first study on transferring vision-language-action (VLA) policies to real greenhouse tabletop strawberry harvesting, a long-horizon, unstructured task challenged by occlusion and specular reflections. We built an end-to-end closed-loop system on the HarvestFlex platform using three-view RGB sensing (two fixed scene views plus a wrist-mounted view) and intentionally avoided depth clouds and explicit geometric calibration. We collected 3.71 h of VR teleoperated demonstrations (227 episodes) and fine-tuned pi_0, pi_0.5, and WALL-OSS with full fine-tuning and LoRA. Under a unified 50 trials real-greenhouse protocol and metrics spanning completion, pi_0.5 with full fine-tuning achieved success rate of 74.0% with 32.6 s/pick and damage rate of 4.1%. Asynchronous inference-control decoupling further improved performance over synchronous deployment. Results showed non-trivial closed-loop picking with fewer than four hours of real data, while remaining limited by close-range observability loss and contact-dynamics mismatch. A demonstration video is available at: https://youtu.be/bN8ZowZKPMI.

AI.tldr: First study transferring vision-language-action policies to real greenhouse strawberry harvesting using VR teleoperated demonstrations and fine-tuning methods, achieving 74% success rate with 32.6 seconds per pick.
AI.motivation: Address the challenge of transferring VLA policies to real-world long-horizon, unstructured tasks like greenhouse strawberry harvesting, which involves occlusion and specular reflections, while avoiding complex depth sensing and calibration.
AI.method: Built end-to-end closed-loop system on HarvestFlex platform using three-view RGB sensing (two fixed scene views plus wrist-mounted view), collected 3.71 hours of VR teleoperated demonstrations (227 episodes), and fine-tuned pi_0, pi_0.5, and WALL-OSS models with both full fine-tuning and LoRA approaches.
AI.result: Under 50-trial real-greenhouse protocol, pi_0.5 with full fine-tuning achieved 74.0% success rate, 32.6 seconds per pick, and 4.1% damage rate. Asynchronous inference-control decoupling further improved performance over synchronous deployment.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrated non-trivial closed-loop picking with less than four hours of real data, showing feasibility of VLA policy transfer to complex real-world tasks, though limitations remain in close-range observability loss and contact-dynamics mismatch.

title: PixARMesh: Autoregressive Mesh-Native Single-View Scene Reconstruction
summary: We introduce PixARMesh, a method to autoregressively reconstruct complete 3D indoor scene meshes directly from a single RGB image. Unlike prior methods that rely on implicit signed distance fields and post-hoc layout optimization, PixARMesh jointly predicts object layout and geometry within a unified model, producing coherent and artist-ready meshes in a single forward pass. Building on recent advances in mesh generative models, we augment a point-cloud encoder with pixel-aligned image features and global scene context via cross-attention, enabling accurate spatial reasoning from a single image. Scenes are generated autoregressively from a unified token stream containing context, pose, and mesh, yielding compact meshes with high-fidelity geometry. Experiments on synthetic and real-world datasets show that PixARMesh achieves state-of-the-art reconstruction quality while producing lightweight, high-quality meshes ready for downstream applications.

AI.tldr: PixARMesh is a single-image 3D scene reconstruction method that generates complete indoor scene meshes autoregressively in one forward pass, producing artist-ready meshes without post-hoc optimization.
AI.motivation: Prior methods for 3D scene reconstruction from single images rely on implicit signed distance fields and require post-hoc layout optimization, which can be inefficient and produce less coherent results. There's a need for a unified approach that jointly predicts object layout and geometry while producing production-ready meshes.
AI.method: PixARMesh uses a point-cloud encoder augmented with pixel-aligned image features and global scene context via cross-attention. It generates scenes autoregressively from a unified token stream containing context, pose, and mesh information, enabling joint prediction of object layout and geometry in a single forward pass.
AI.result: Experiments on synthetic and real-world datasets show PixARMesh achieves state-of-the-art reconstruction quality while producing lightweight, high-quality meshes. The method generates compact meshes with high-fidelity geometry that are ready for downstream applications.
AI.conclusion: PixARMesh represents a significant advance in single-image 3D scene reconstruction by producing complete, coherent, artist-ready meshes in a unified autoregressive framework, outperforming prior methods that require separate layout optimization steps.

title: Stock Market Prediction Using Node Transformer Architecture Integrated with BERT Sentiment Analysis
comment: 14 pages, 5 figures, 10 tables, submitted to IEEE Access
summary: Stock market prediction presents considerable challenges for investors, financial institutions, and policymakers operating in complex market environments characterized by noise, non-stationarity, and behavioral dynamics. Traditional forecasting methods often fail to capture the intricate patterns and cross-sectional dependencies inherent in financial markets. This paper presents an integrated framework combining a node transformer architecture with BERT-based sentiment analysis for stock price forecasting. The proposed model represents the stock market as a graph structure where individual stocks form nodes and edges capture relationships including sectoral affiliations, correlated price movements, and supply chain connections. A fine-tuned BERT model extracts sentiment from social media posts and combines it with quantitative market features through attention-based fusion. The node transformer processes historical market data while capturing both temporal evolution and cross-sectional dependencies among stocks. Experiments on 20 S&P 500 stocks spanning January 1982 to March 2025 demonstrate that the integrated model achieves a mean absolute percentage error (MAPE) of 0.80% for one-day-ahead predictions, compared to 1.20% for ARIMA and 1.00% for LSTM. Sentiment analysis reduces prediction error by 10% overall and 25% during earnings announcements, while graph-based modeling contributes an additional 15% improvement by capturing inter-stock dependencies. Directional accuracy reaches 65% for one-day forecasts. Statistical validation through paired t-tests confirms these improvements (p < 0.05 for all comparisons). The model maintains MAPE below 1.5% during high-volatility periods where baseline models exceed 2%.

AI.tldr: Integrated framework combining node transformer architecture with BERT-based sentiment analysis for stock price forecasting, achieving 0.80% MAPE for one-day-ahead predictions with 65% directional accuracy.
AI.motivation: Stock market prediction is challenging due to noise, non-stationarity, and behavioral dynamics. Traditional methods fail to capture intricate patterns and cross-sectional dependencies in financial markets.
AI.method: Proposes an integrated framework representing stock market as a graph structure (stocks as nodes, relationships as edges). Uses fine-tuned BERT for sentiment analysis from social media, combined with quantitative features via attention-based fusion. Node transformer processes historical data capturing both temporal evolution and cross-sectional dependencies.
AI.result: Achieves 0.80% MAPE for one-day-ahead predictions vs 1.20% for ARIMA and 1.00% for LSTM. Sentiment analysis reduces error by 10% overall (25% during earnings). Graph-based modeling adds 15% improvement. Directional accuracy reaches 65%. Statistical validation confirms improvements (p < 0.05). Maintains MAPE below 1.5% during high-volatility periods.
AI.conclusion: The integrated framework combining graph-based modeling with sentiment analysis significantly improves stock price forecasting accuracy, particularly during volatile periods and earnings announcements, demonstrating the value of capturing both structural dependencies and behavioral factors.

title: MagRobot:An Open Simulator for Magnetically Navigated Robots
summary: Magnetic navigation systems, including magnetic tracking systems and magnetic actuation systems, have shown great potential for occlusion-free localization and remote control of intracorporeal medical devices and robots in minimally invasive medicine, such as capsule endoscopy and cardiovascular intervention. However, the design of magnetically navigated robots remains heavily reliant on experimental prototyping, which is time-consuming and costly. Furthermore, there is a lack of a consistent experimental environment to compare and benchmark the hardware and algorithms across different magnetic navigation systems. To address these challenges, we propose the first universal open-source simulation platform to facilitate research, design and benchmarking of magnetically navigated robots. Our simulator features an intuitive graphical user interface that enables the user to efficiently design, visualize, and analyze magnetic navigation systems for both rigid and soft robots. The proposed simulator is versatile, which can simulate both magnetic actuation and magnetic tracking tasks in diverse medical applications that involve deformable anatomies. The proposed simulator provides an open development environment, where the user can load third-party anatomical models and customize both hardware and algorithms of magnetic navigation systems. The fidelity of the simulator is validated using both phantom and ex vivo experiments of magnetic navigation of a continuum robot and a capsule robot with diverse magnetic actuation setups. Three use cases of the simulator, i.e., bronchoscopy, endovascular intervention, and gastrointestinal endoscopy, are implemented to demonstrate the functionality of the simulator. It is shown that the configuration and algorithms of magnetic navigation systems can be flexibly designed and optimized for better performance using the simulator.

AI.tldr: First universal open-source simulation platform for designing, benchmarking, and optimizing magnetically navigated medical robots, validated with phantom/ex vivo experiments.
AI.motivation: Current magnetic navigation system design relies heavily on experimental prototyping (time-consuming, costly) and lacks consistent experimental environments for benchmarking hardware/algorithms across different systems.
AI.method: Developed an intuitive GUI-based simulator that can simulate both magnetic actuation and tracking tasks for rigid/soft robots, supports third-party anatomical models, and allows customization of hardware/algorithms.
AI.result: Simulator validated using phantom/ex vivo experiments with continuum and capsule robots; demonstrated functionality through three medical use cases (bronchoscopy, endovascular intervention, GI endoscopy); enables flexible design/optimization of magnetic navigation systems.
AI.conclusion: The universal open-source simulation platform addresses key challenges in magnetic navigation research by providing a consistent benchmarking environment and reducing reliance on expensive experimental prototyping.

title: InnoAds-Composer: Efficient Condition Composition for E-Commerce Poster Generation
summary: E-commerce product poster generation aims to automatically synthesize a single image that effectively conveys product information by presenting a subject, text, and a designed style. Recent diffusion models with fine-grained and efficient controllability have advanced product poster synthesis, yet they typically rely on multi-stage pipelines, and simultaneous control over subject, text, and style remains underexplored. Such naive multi-stage pipelines also show three issues: poor subject fidelity, inaccurate text, and inconsistent style. To address these issues, we propose InnoAds-Composer, a single-stage framework that enables efficient tri-conditional control tokens over subject, glyph, and style. To alleviate the quadratic overhead introduced by naive tri-conditional token concatenation, we perform importance analysis over layers and timesteps and route each condition only to the most responsive positions, thereby shortening the active token sequence. Besides, to improve the accuracy of Chinese text rendering, we design a Text Feature Enhancement Module (TFEM) that integrates features from both glyph images and glyph crops. To support training and evaluation, we also construct a high-quality e-commerce product poster dataset and benchmark, which is the first dataset that jointly contains subject, text, and style conditions. Extensive experiments demonstrate that InnoAds-Composer significantly outperforms existing product poster methods without obviously increasing inference latency.

AI.tldr: InnoAds-Composer: A single-stage diffusion framework for e-commerce poster generation with efficient tri-conditional control over subject, text, and style, addressing issues in multi-stage pipelines.
AI.motivation: Current diffusion models for product poster generation rely on multi-stage pipelines with poor subject fidelity, inaccurate text rendering, and inconsistent style control. There's a need for simultaneous control over all three elements (subject, text, style) in a more efficient framework.
AI.method: Proposes InnoAds-Composer, a single-stage framework with tri-conditional control tokens. Uses importance analysis over layers and timesteps to route each condition only to most responsive positions, reducing token sequence length. Includes Text Feature Enhancement Module (TFEM) for better Chinese text rendering by integrating glyph image and crop features.
AI.result: Significantly outperforms existing product poster methods without increasing inference latency. Also contributes a new high-quality e-commerce poster dataset with subject, text, and style conditions - the first of its kind.
AI.conclusion: InnoAds-Composer effectively addresses the limitations of multi-stage pipelines by enabling efficient simultaneous control over subject, text, and style in product poster generation, while maintaining computational efficiency.

title: Design Experiments to Compare Multi-armed Bandit Algorithms
summary: Online platforms routinely compare multi-armed bandit algorithms, such as UCB and Thompson Sampling, to select the best-performing policy. Unlike standard A/B tests for static treatments, each run of a bandit algorithm over $T$ users produces only one dependent trajectory, because the algorithm's decisions depend on all past interactions. Reliable inference therefore demands many independent restarts of the algorithm, making experimentation costly and delaying deployment decisions. We propose Artificial Replay (AR) as a new experimental design for this problem. AR first runs one policy and records its trajectory. When the second policy is executed, it reuses a recorded reward whenever it selects an action the first policy already took, and queries the real environment only otherwise. We develop a new analytical framework for this design and prove three key properties of the resulting estimator: it is unbiased; it requires only $T + o(T)$ user interactions instead of $2T$ for a run of the treatment and control policies, nearly halving the experimental cost when both policies have sub-linear regret; and its variance grows sub-linearly in $T$, whereas the estimator from a naïve design has a linearly-growing variance. Numerical experiments with UCB, Thompson Sampling, and $ε$-greedy policies confirm these theoretical gains.

AI.tldr: Artificial Replay (AR) is a new experimental design for comparing bandit algorithms that reuses recorded rewards from one policy to evaluate another, reducing experimental cost and variance.
AI.motivation: Comparing multi-armed bandit algorithms requires many independent restarts due to dependent trajectories, making experimentation costly and delaying deployment decisions. Standard A/B testing approaches are inefficient for bandit algorithms.
AI.method: AR runs one policy and records its trajectory. When evaluating a second policy, it reuses recorded rewards when the second policy selects actions already taken by the first policy, and only queries the real environment for new actions.
AI.result: The AR estimator is unbiased, requires only T + o(T) user interactions instead of 2T (nearly halving experimental cost), and has sub-linearly growing variance compared to linearly-growing variance in naive designs.
AI.conclusion: Artificial Replay provides an efficient experimental design for comparing bandit algorithms with theoretical guarantees and practical benefits, as demonstrated with UCB, Thompson Sampling, and ε-greedy policies.

title: Moving Through Clutter: Scaling Data Collection and Benchmarking for 3D Scene-Aware Humanoid Locomotion via Virtual Reality
summary: Recent advances in humanoid locomotion have enabled dynamic behaviors such as dancing, martial arts, and parkour, yet these capabilities are predominantly demonstrated in open, flat, and obstacle-free settings. In contrast, real-world environments such as homes, offices, and public spaces, are densely cluttered, three-dimensional, and geometrically constrained, requiring scene-aware whole-body coordination, precise balance control, and reasoning over spatial constraints imposed by furniture and household objects. However, humanoid locomotion in cluttered 3D environments remains underexplored, and no public dataset systematically couples full-body human locomotion with the scene geometry that shapes it. To address this gap, we present Moving Through Clutter (MTC), an opensource Virtual Reality (VR) based data collection and evaluation framework for scene-aware humanoid locomotion in cluttered environments. Our system procedurally generates scenes with controllable clutter levels and captures embodiment-consistent, whole-body human motion through immersive VR navigation, which is then automatically retargeted to a humanoid robot model. We further introduce benchmarks that quantify environment clutter level and locomotion performance, including stability and collision safety. Using this framework, we compile a dataset of 348 trajectories across 145 diverse 3D cluttered scenes. The dataset provides a foundation for studying geometry-induced adaptation in humanoid locomotion and developing scene-aware planning and control methods.

AI.tldr: MTC is a VR-based framework for collecting scene-aware humanoid locomotion data in cluttered 3D environments, addressing the gap between current humanoid capabilities in open spaces and real-world cluttered settings.
AI.motivation: Current humanoid locomotion research focuses on open, flat environments, but real-world settings (homes, offices) are densely cluttered and geometrically constrained, requiring scene-aware whole-body coordination. There's no public dataset coupling full-body human locomotion with scene geometry.
AI.method: Developed Moving Through Clutter (MTC): a VR-based data collection framework that procedurally generates cluttered scenes with controllable clutter levels, captures whole-body human motion through immersive VR navigation, and automatically retargets motion to humanoid robot models.
AI.result: Created a dataset of 348 trajectories across 145 diverse 3D cluttered scenes, with benchmarks quantifying environment clutter level and locomotion performance (stability and collision safety).
AI.conclusion: MTC provides a foundation for studying geometry-induced adaptation in humanoid locomotion and developing scene-aware planning and control methods, addressing the underexplored challenge of humanoid navigation in cluttered 3D environments.

title: Mitigating Bias in Concept Bottleneck Models for Fair and Interpretable Image Classification
summary: Ensuring fairness in image classification prevents models from perpetuating and amplifying bias. Concept bottleneck models (CBMs) map images to high-level, human-interpretable concepts before making predictions via a sparse, one-layer classifier. This structure enhances interpretability and, in theory, supports fairness by masking sensitive attribute proxies such as facial features. However, CBM concepts have been known to leak information unrelated to concept semantics and early results reveal only marginal reductions in gender bias on datasets like ImSitu. We propose three bias mitigation techniques to improve fairness in CBMs: 1. Decreasing information leakage using a top-k concept filter, 2. Removing biased concepts, and 3. Adversarial debiasing. Our results outperform prior work in terms of fairness-performance tradeoffs, indicating that our debiased CBM provides a significant step towards fair and interpretable image classification.

AI.tldr: Three bias mitigation techniques for concept bottleneck models improve fairness in image classification while maintaining interpretability.
AI.motivation: Concept bottleneck models (CBMs) theoretically support fairness by masking sensitive attributes, but in practice they leak information and show only marginal bias reduction. There's a need to improve fairness in CBMs while preserving their interpretability benefits.
AI.method: Three bias mitigation techniques: 1) Top-k concept filter to decrease information leakage, 2) Removal of biased concepts, and 3) Adversarial debiasing. These are applied to concept bottleneck models for fair image classification.
AI.result: Outperforms prior work in fairness-performance tradeoffs, providing significant improvements over previous CBMs that showed only marginal gender bias reduction on datasets like ImSitu.
AI.conclusion: The proposed debiased CBM represents a significant step toward achieving both fair and interpretable image classification systems.

title: Weak-SIGReg: Covariance Regularization for Stable Deep Learning
comment: Accepted at GRaM workshop (ICLR 2026). Code & supplementary: https://github.com/kreasof-ai/sigreg
summary: Modern neural network optimization relies heavily on architectural priorssuch as Batch Normalization and Residual connectionsto stabilize training dynamics. Without these, or in low-data regimes with aggressive augmentation, low-bias architectures like Vision Transformers (ViTs) often suffer from optimization collapse. This work adopts Sketched Isotropic Gaussian Regularization (SIGReg), recently introduced in the LeJEPA self-supervised framework, and repurposes it as a general optimization stabilizer for supervised learning. While the original formulation targets the full characteristic function, a computationally efficient variant is derived, Weak-SIGReg, which targets the covariance matrix via random sketching. Inspired by interacting particle systems, representation collapse is viewed as stochastic drift; SIGReg constrains the representation density towards an isotropic Gaussian, mitigating this drift. Empirically, SIGReg recovers the training of a ViT on CIFAR-100 from a collapsed 20.73\% to 72.02\% accuracy without architectural hacks and significantly improves the convergence of deep vanilla MLPs trained with pure SGD. Code is available at \href{https://github.com/kreasof-ai/sigreg}{github.com/kreasof-ai/sigreg}.

AI.tldr: SIGReg repurposed from self-supervised learning to stabilize supervised optimization by constraining representations towards isotropic Gaussian, preventing collapse in low-bias architectures like ViTs.
AI.motivation: Modern neural networks rely on architectural priors (BatchNorm, Residual connections) for stable training. Without these, or in low-data regimes with aggressive augmentation, low-bias architectures like Vision Transformers suffer from optimization collapse.
AI.method: Adopts Sketched Isotropic Gaussian Regularization (SIGReg) from LeJEPA framework and repurposes it for supervised learning. Derives computationally efficient Weak-SIGReg variant that targets covariance matrix via random sketching. Views representation collapse as stochastic drift and constrains representation density towards isotropic Gaussian.
AI.result: SIGRec recovers ViT training on CIFAR-100 from collapsed 20.73% to 72.02% accuracy without architectural hacks. Significantly improves convergence of deep vanilla MLPs trained with pure SGD.
AI.conclusion: SIGReg serves as an effective general optimization stabilizer for supervised learning, particularly beneficial for low-bias architectures and challenging training regimes where traditional architectural priors are insufficient.

title: TADPO: Reinforcement Learning Goes Off-road
comment: 8 pages, 5 figures, 2 tables. Accepted at ICRA 2026
summary: Off-road autonomous driving poses significant challenges such as navigating unmapped, variable terrain with uncertain and diverse dynamics. Addressing these challenges requires effective long-horizon planning and adaptable control. Reinforcement Learning (RL) offers a promising solution by learning control policies directly from interaction. However, because off-road driving is a long-horizon task with low-signal rewards, standard RL methods are challenging to apply in this setting. We introduce TADPO, a novel policy gradient formulation that extends Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO), leveraging off-policy trajectories for teacher guidance and on-policy trajectories for student exploration. Building on this, we develop a vision-based, end-to-end RL system for high-speed off-road driving, capable of navigating extreme slopes and obstacle-rich terrain. We demonstrate our performance in simulation and, importantly, zero-shot sim-to-real transfer on a full-scale off-road vehicle. To our knowledge, this work represents the first deployment of RL-based policies on a full-scale off-road platform.

AI.tldr: TADPO: A novel policy gradient method extending PPO for off-road autonomous driving, combining off-policy teacher guidance with on-policy student exploration for effective long-horizon planning.
AI.motivation: Off-road autonomous driving faces challenges with unmapped, variable terrain and uncertain dynamics requiring long-horizon planning. Standard RL methods struggle due to long-horizon tasks with low-signal rewards.
AI.method: TADPO extends PPO by leveraging off-policy trajectories for teacher guidance and on-policy trajectories for student exploration. Developed a vision-based, end-to-end RL system for high-speed off-road driving.
AI.result: Demonstrated performance in simulation and achieved zero-shot sim-to-real transfer on a full-scale off-road vehicle, navigating extreme slopes and obstacle-rich terrain.
AI.conclusion: First deployment of RL-based policies on a full-scale off-road platform, showing TADPO's effectiveness for challenging off-road autonomous driving tasks.

title: CollabOD: Collaborative Multi-Backbone with Cross-scale Vision for UAV Small Object Detection
summary: Small object detection in unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) imagery is challenging, mainly due to scale variation, structural detail degradation, and limited computational resources. In high-altitude scenarios, fine-grained features are further weakened during hierarchical downsampling and cross-scale fusion, resulting in unstable localization and reduced robustness. To address this issue, we propose CollabOD, a lightweight collaborative detection framework that explicitly preserves structural details and aligns heterogeneous feature streams before multi-scale fusion. The framework integrates Structural Detail Preservation, Cross-Path Feature Alignment, and Localization-Aware Lightweight Design strategies. From the perspectives of image processing, channel structure, and lightweight design, it optimizes the architecture of conventional UAV perception models. The proposed design enhances representation stability while maintaining efficient inference. A unified detail-aware detection head further improves regression robustness without introducing additional deployment overhead. The code is available at: https://github.com/Bai-Xuecheng/CollabOD.

AI.tldr: CollabOD is a lightweight collaborative detection framework for small object detection in UAV imagery that preserves structural details and aligns features before fusion to address scale variation and detail degradation issues.
AI.motivation: Small object detection in UAV imagery faces challenges from scale variation, structural detail degradation, and limited computational resources. In high-altitude scenarios, fine-grained features are weakened during downsampling and cross-scale fusion, leading to unstable localization and reduced robustness.
AI.method: Proposes CollabOD framework with three key strategies: Structural Detail Preservation, Cross-Path Feature Alignment, and Localization-Aware Lightweight Design. Integrates these approaches to optimize conventional UAV perception models from image processing, channel structure, and lightweight design perspectives. Includes a unified detail-aware detection head for improved regression robustness.
AI.result: The framework enhances representation stability while maintaining efficient inference. The detail-aware detection head improves regression robustness without adding deployment overhead. Code is publicly available on GitHub.
AI.conclusion: CollabOD effectively addresses small object detection challenges in UAV imagery by preserving structural details, aligning heterogeneous features, and maintaining lightweight design for practical deployment in resource-constrained scenarios.

title: Omni-Masked Gradient Descent: Memory-Efficient Optimization via Mask Traversal with Improved Convergence
summary: Memory-efficient optimization methods have recently gained increasing attention for scaling full-parameter training of large language models under the GPU-memory bottleneck. Existing approaches either lack clear convergence guarantees, or only achieve the standard ${\mathcal{O}}(ε^{-4})$ iteration complexity in the nonconvex settings. We propose Omni-Masked Gradient Descent (OMGD), an optimization method based on mask traversal for memory efficient training, and provide a nonconvex convergence analysis that establishes a strictly improved iteration complexity of $\tilde{\mathcal{O}}(ε^{-3})$ for finding an $ε$-approximate stationary point. Empirically, OMGD is a lightweight, plug-and-play approach that integrates seamlessly into most mainstream optimizers, yielding consistent improvements over competitive baselines in both fine-tuning and pre-training tasks.

AI.tldr: OMGD is a memory-efficient optimization method for large language models that achieves better theoretical convergence (Õ(ε⁻³)) than standard methods (O(ε⁻⁴)) while being lightweight and plug-and-play.
AI.motivation: Current memory-efficient optimization methods for large language models either lack convergence guarantees or only achieve suboptimal O(ε⁻⁴) iteration complexity, creating a need for methods with both theoretical guarantees and practical efficiency.
AI.method: Omni-Masked Gradient Descent (OMGD) uses mask traversal for memory-efficient training, allowing it to be integrated as a lightweight, plug-and-play component into most mainstream optimizers.
AI.result: OMGD achieves a strictly improved iteration complexity of Õ(ε⁻³) for finding ε-approximate stationary points, and empirically shows consistent improvements over competitive baselines in both fine-tuning and pre-training tasks.
AI.conclusion: OMGD provides a theoretically sound and practically effective solution for memory-efficient training of large language models, offering better convergence guarantees while maintaining ease of integration with existing optimizers.

title: Restoring Linguistic Grounding in VLA Models via Train-Free Attention Recalibration
summary: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models enable robots to perform manipulation tasks directly from natural language instructions and are increasingly viewed as a foundation for generalist robotic policies. However, their reliability under Out-of-Distribution (OOD) instructions remains underexplored. In this paper, we reveal a critical failure mode in which VLA policies continue executing visually plausible actions even when the language instruction contradicts the scene. We refer to this phenomenon as linguistic blindness, where VLA policies prioritize visual priors over instruction semantics during action generation. To systematically analyze this issue, we introduce ICBench, a diagnostic benchmark constructed from the LIBERO dataset that probes language-action coupling by injecting controlled OOD instruction contradictions while keeping the visual environment unchanged. Evaluations on three representative VLA architectures, including Pi0, Pi0.5 and OpenVLA OFT, show that these models frequently succeed at tasks despite logically impossible instructions, revealing a strong visual bias in action generation. To mitigate this issue, we propose Instruction-Guided Attention Recalibration (IGAR), a train-free inference-time mechanism that rebalances attention distributions to restore the influence of language instructions. IGAR operates without retraining or architectural modification and can be directly applied to existing VLA models. Experiments across 30 LIBERO tasks demonstrate that IGAR substantially reduces erroneous execution under OOD contradictory instructions while preserving baseline task performance. We additionally validate the approach on a real Franka robotic arm, where IGAR effectively prevents manipulation triggered by inconsistent instructions.

AI.tldr: VLA models suffer from "linguistic blindness" - they prioritize visual cues over contradictory language instructions, executing plausible but wrong actions. The paper introduces ICBench to diagnose this and proposes IGAR, a train-free attention recalibration method to fix it.
AI.motivation: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models are becoming foundation models for robotic policies, but their reliability under Out-of-Distribution (OOD) instructions is underexplored. The paper identifies a critical failure mode where VLA policies execute visually plausible actions even when language instructions contradict the scene, calling this "linguistic blindness."
AI.method: 1) Introduces ICBench, a diagnostic benchmark from LIBERO dataset that probes language-action coupling by injecting controlled OOD instruction contradictions while keeping visual environment unchanged. 2) Proposes Instruction-Guided Attention Recalibration (IGAR), a train-free inference-time mechanism that rebalances attention distributions to restore language instruction influence without retraining or architectural modifications.
AI.result: Evaluations on three VLA architectures (Pi0, Pi0.5, OpenVLA OFT) show models frequently succeed at tasks despite logically impossible instructions, revealing strong visual bias. IGAR substantially reduces erroneous execution under OOD contradictory instructions while preserving baseline task performance across 30 LIBERO tasks. Real-world validation on Franka robotic arm shows IGAR effectively prevents manipulation triggered by inconsistent instructions.
AI.conclusion: VLA models exhibit linguistic blindness where visual priors dominate instruction semantics. The proposed IGAR method effectively mitigates this issue through attention recalibration at inference time, improving reliability under contradictory instructions without compromising baseline performance or requiring model retraining.

title: Beyond Geometry: Artistic Disparity Synthesis for Immersive 2D-to-3D
summary: Current 2D-to-3D conversion methods achieve geometric accuracy but are artistically deficient, failing to replicate the immersive and emotionally resonant experience of professional 3D cinema. This is because geometric reconstruction paradigms mistake deliberate artistic intent, such as strategic zero-plane shifts for pop-out effects and local depth sculpting, for data noise or ambiguity. This paper argues for a new paradigm: Artistic Disparity Synthesis, shifting the goal from physically accurate disparity estimation to artistically coherent disparity synthesis. We propose Art3D, a preliminary framework exploring this paradigm. Art3D uses a dual-path architecture to decouple global depth parameters (macro-intent) from local artistic effects (visual brushstrokes) and learns from professional 3D film data via indirect supervision. We also introduce a preliminary evaluation method to quantify cinematic alignment. Experiments show our approach demonstrates potential in replicating key local out-of-screen effects and aligning with the global depth styles of cinematic 3D content, laying the groundwork for a new class of artistically-driven conversion tools.

AI.tldr: Art3D proposes artistic disparity synthesis for 3D conversion, shifting from geometric accuracy to artistic coherence by learning from professional 3D films to replicate cinematic depth effects.
AI.motivation: Current 2D-to-3D conversion methods achieve geometric accuracy but lack artistic quality, failing to replicate the immersive and emotionally resonant experience of professional 3D cinema. They mistake deliberate artistic intent (like zero-plane shifts for pop-out effects) for data noise or ambiguity.
AI.method: Art3D uses a dual-path architecture to decouple global depth parameters (macro-intent) from local artistic effects (visual brushstrokes), learning from professional 3D film data via indirect supervision. It introduces Artistic Disparity Synthesis paradigm instead of physically accurate disparity estimation.
AI.result: Experiments show Art3D demonstrates potential in replicating key local out-of-screen effects and aligning with the global depth styles of cinematic 3D content, laying groundwork for artistically-driven conversion tools.
AI.conclusion: The paper argues for a paradigm shift from geometric reconstruction to artistic disparity synthesis, proposing Art3D as a preliminary framework that shows promise in creating more artistically coherent 3D conversions that capture professional cinematic intent.

title: EvoESAP: Non-Uniform Expert Pruning for Sparse MoE
summary: Sparse Mixture-of-Experts (SMoE) language models achieve strong capability at low per-token compute, yet deployment remains memory- and throughput-bound because the full expert pool must be stored and served. Post-training expert pruning reduces this cost, but most methods focus on which experts to prune within each layer and default to a uniform layer-wise sparsity allocation, even though the allocation can strongly affect performance. We decouple pruning into within-layer expert ranking and across-layer budget allocation, and introduce \textbf{E}xpected \textbf{S}peculative \textbf{A}cceptance \textbf{P}roxy (\textbf{ESAP}), a speculative-decoding-inspired, teacher-forced metric that measures how well a pruned model matches the full model. ESAP is bounded and stable, enabling cheap comparison of many candidates without costly autoregressive decoding. Building on ESAP, we propose EvoESAP, an evolutionary searching framework that optimizes a non-uniform layer-wise sparsity allocation under a fixed global budget while holding the within-layer pruning order fixed, making it a plug-and-play method with criteria such as Frequency, EAN, SEER, and REAP. Across 7B--30B SMoE LLMs at 25\% and 50\% sparsity, EvoESAP consistently discovers non-uniform allocations that improve open-ended generation (up to \textbf{+19.6\%} on MATH-500 at 50\% sparsity) while preserving competitive multiple-choice accuracy compared with uniform pruning at the same sparsity.

AI.tldr: EvoESAP: Evolutionary search framework using ESAP metric to optimize non-uniform expert pruning allocations in SMoE models, improving generation quality while maintaining accuracy.
AI.motivation: Current SMoE pruning methods focus on which experts to prune within layers but use uniform layer-wise sparsity allocation, even though allocation strongly affects performance. Need better allocation strategies to reduce memory/throughput costs while preserving model quality.
AI.method: Decouple pruning into within-layer expert ranking and across-layer budget allocation. Introduce ESAP (Expected Speculative Acceptance Proxy) metric to measure how well pruned models match full models. Use EvoESAP evolutionary search framework to optimize non-uniform layer-wise sparsity allocations under fixed global budget.
AI.result: Across 7B-30B SMoE LLMs at 25% and 50% sparsity, EvoESAP discovers non-uniform allocations that improve open-ended generation (up to +19.6% on MATH-500 at 50% sparsity) while preserving competitive multiple-choice accuracy compared to uniform pruning.
AI.conclusion: EvoESAP provides effective plug-and-play method for optimizing expert pruning allocations, demonstrating that non-uniform sparsity allocation significantly improves SMoE model performance while maintaining efficiency benefits.

title: RODEO: RObotic DEcentralized Organization
comment: 8 pages, 6 figures, Accepted at IEEE International Conference on Robotics & Automation (2026)
summary: Robots are improving their autonomy with minimal human supervision. However, auditable actions, transparent decision processes, and new human-robot interaction models are still missing requirements to achieve extended robot autonomy. To tackle these challenges, we propose RODEO (RObotic DEcentralized Organization), a blockchain-based framework that integrates trust and accountability mechanisms for robots. This paper formalizes Decentralized Autonomous Organizations (DAOs) for service robots. First, it provides a ROS-ETH bridge between the DAO and the robots. Second, it offers templates that enable organizations (e.g., companies, universities) to integrate service robots into their operations. Third, it provides proof-verification mechanisms that allow robot actions to be auditable. In our experimental setup, a mobile robot was deployed as a trash collector in a lab scenario. The robot collects trash and uses a smart bin to sort and dispose of it correctly. Then, the robot submits a proof of the successful operation and is compensated in DAO tokens. Finally, the robot re-invests the acquired funds to purchase battery charging services. Data collected in a three day experiment show that the robot doubled its income and reinvested funds to extend its operating time. The proof validation times of approximately one minute ensured verifiable task execution, while the accumulated robot income successfully funded up to 88 hours of future autonomous operation. The results of this research give insights about how robots and organizations can coordinate tasks and payments with auditable execution proofs and on-chain settlement.

AI.tldr: RODEO is a blockchain-based framework that enables service robots to operate as Decentralized Autonomous Organizations (DAOs) with trust, accountability, and auditable actions through smart contracts and proof-verification mechanisms.
AI.motivation: Current robot autonomy lacks auditable actions, transparent decision processes, and proper human-robot interaction models needed for extended autonomy. There's a need for trust and accountability mechanisms in robotic systems.
AI.method: Proposed RODEO framework with: 1) ROS-ETH bridge connecting robots to DAOs, 2) templates for organizations to integrate service robots, 3) proof-verification mechanisms for auditable robot actions. Tested with mobile trash collector robot using smart bin and blockchain-based compensation system.
AI.result: In 3-day experiment: robot doubled its income, reinvested funds for battery charging, achieved ~1 minute proof validation times, funded up to 88 hours of future autonomous operation through accumulated income.
AI.conclusion: Blockchain-based DAOs enable robots and organizations to coordinate tasks and payments with auditable execution proofs and on-chain settlement, providing trust, accountability, and extended autonomy for service robots.

title: Pano3DComposer: Feed-Forward Compositional 3D Scene Generation from Single Panoramic Image
summary: Current compositional image-to-3D scene generation approaches construct 3D scenes by time-consuming iterative layout optimization or inflexible joint object-layout generation. Moreover, most methods rely on limited field-of-view perspective images, hindering the creation of complete 360-degree environments. To address these limitations, we design Pano3DComposer, an efficient feed-forward framework for panoramic images. To decouple object generation from layout estimation, we propose a plug-and-play Object-World Transformation Predictor. This module converts the 3D objects generated by off-the-shelf image-to-3D models from local to world coordinates. To achieve this, we adapt the VGGT architecture to Alignment-VGGT by using target object crop, multi-view object renderings and camera parameters to predict the transformation. The predictor is trained using pseudo-geometric supervision to address the shape discrepancy between generated and ground-truth objects. For input images from unseen domains, we further introduce a Coarse-to-Fine (C2F) alignment mechanism for Pano3DComposer that iteratively refines geometric consistency with feedback of scene rendering. Our method achieves superior geometric accuracy for image/text-to-3D tasks on synthetic and real-world datasets. It can generate a high-fidelity 3D scene in approximately 20 seconds on an RTX 4090 GPU. Project page: https://qiuzidian.github.io/pano3dcomposer-page/.

AI.tldr: Pano3DComposer is a feed-forward framework that efficiently generates 360-degree 3D scenes from panoramic images by decoupling object generation from layout estimation using an Object-World Transformation Predictor.
AI.motivation: Current compositional image-to-3D scene generation methods are either too slow (iterative optimization) or inflexible (joint object-layout generation), and most rely on limited field-of-view perspective images, preventing creation of complete 360-degree environments.
AI.method: Proposes Pano3DComposer with: 1) Object-World Transformation Predictor that converts 3D objects from off-the-shelf models from local to world coordinates using Alignment-VGGT architecture; 2) Pseudo-geometric supervision to handle shape discrepancies; 3) Coarse-to-Fine alignment mechanism for unseen domains that iteratively refines geometric consistency.
AI.result: Achieves superior geometric accuracy for image/text-to-3D tasks on synthetic and real-world datasets. Can generate high-fidelity 3D scenes in approximately 20 seconds on an RTX 4090 GPU.
AI.conclusion: Pano3DComposer provides an efficient, feed-forward solution for panoramic 3D scene generation that addresses limitations of existing methods through decoupled object-layout processing and robust transformation prediction.

title: Preventing Learning Stagnation in PPO by Scaling to 1 Million Parallel Environments
summary: Plateaus, where an agent's performance stagnates at a suboptimal level, are a common problem in deep on-policy RL. Focusing on PPO due to its widespread adoption, we show that plateaus in certain regimes arise not because of known exploration, capacity, or optimization challenges, but because sample-based estimates of the loss eventually become poor proxies for the true objective over the course of training. As a recap, PPO switches between sampling rollouts from several parallel environments online using the current policy (which we call the outer loop) and performing repeated minibatch SGD steps against this offline dataset (the inner loop). In our work we consider only the outer loop, and conceptually model it as stochastic optimization. The step size is then controlled by the regularization strength towards the previous policy and the gradient noise by the number of samples collected between policy update steps. This model predicts that performance will plateau at a suboptimal level if the outer step size is too large relative to the noise. Recasting PPO in this light makes it clear that there are two ways to address this particular type of learning stagnation: either reduce the step size or increase the number of samples collected between updates. We first validate the predictions of our model and investigate how hyperparameter choices influence the step size and update noise, concluding that increasing the number of parallel environments is a simple and robust way to reduce both factors. Next, we propose a recipe for how to co-scale the other hyperparameters when increasing parallelization, and show that incorrectly doing so can lead to severe performance degradation. Finally, we vastly outperform prior baselines in a complex open-ended domain by scaling PPO to more than 1M parallel environments, thereby enabling monotonic performance improvement up to one trillion transitions.

AI.tldr: PPO performance plateaus stem from noisy gradient estimates, not exploration or optimization issues. Increasing parallel environments reduces noise and enables scaling to 1M+ environments with monotonic improvement up to 1T transitions.
AI.motivation: Address performance plateaus in deep on-policy RL (specifically PPO) that occur not from known challenges like exploration or capacity, but from sample-based loss estimates becoming poor proxies for the true objective during training.
AI.method: Model PPO's outer loop as stochastic optimization where step size is controlled by regularization strength and gradient noise by samples between updates. Validate model predictions, analyze hyperparameter effects, propose co-scaling recipe for parallelization, and scale PPO to over 1M parallel environments.
AI.result: Increasing parallel environments reduces both step size and update noise. Proper co-scaling of hyperparameters with parallelization is crucial to avoid performance degradation. Achieved state-of-the-art performance by scaling PPO to >1M parallel environments, enabling monotonic improvement up to 1 trillion transitions.
AI.conclusion: PPO plateaus arise from noisy gradient estimates, which can be addressed by increasing parallel environments and properly co-scaling hyperparameters. This approach enables unprecedented scaling and performance in complex open-ended domains.

title: Lifelong Embodied Navigation Learning
summary: Embodied navigation agents powered by large language models have shown strong performance on individual tasks but struggle to continually acquire new navigation skills, which suffer from catastrophic forgetting. We formalize this challenge as lifelong embodied navigation learning (LENL), where an agent is required to adapt to a sequence of navigation tasks spanning multiple scenes and diverse user instruction styles, while retaining previously learned knowledge. To tackle this problem, we propose Uni-Walker, a lifelong embodied navigation framework that decouples navigation knowledge into task-shared and task-specific components with Decoder Extension LoRA (DE-LoRA). To learn the shared knowledge, we design a knowledge inheritance strategy and an experts co-activation strategy to facilitate shared knowledge transfer and refinement across multiple navigation tasks. To learn the specific knowledge, we propose an expert subspace orthogonality constraint together and a navigation-specific chain-of-thought reasoning mechanism to capture specific knowledge and enhance instruction-style understanding. Extensive experiments demonstrate the superiority of Uni-Walker for building universal navigation agents with lifelong learning.

AI.tldr: Uni-Walker: A lifelong embodied navigation framework using Decoder Extension LoRA to decouple navigation knowledge into task-shared and task-specific components, enabling continual learning without catastrophic forgetting.
AI.motivation: Current embodied navigation agents powered by large language models perform well on individual tasks but suffer from catastrophic forgetting when learning new navigation skills sequentially, limiting their ability to continually acquire new knowledge across multiple scenes and instruction styles.
AI.method: Proposes Uni-Walker framework with Decoder Extension LoRA (DE-LoRA) to decouple knowledge into task-shared and task-specific components. Uses knowledge inheritance and experts co-activation strategies for shared knowledge, and expert subspace orthogonality constraint with navigation-specific chain-of-thought reasoning for specific knowledge.
AI.result: Extensive experiments demonstrate Uni-Walker's superiority in building universal navigation agents with lifelong learning capabilities, effectively addressing catastrophic forgetting in sequential navigation task learning.
AI.conclusion: Uni-Walker successfully tackles the lifelong embodied navigation learning challenge by separating shared and specific knowledge components, enabling agents to continually adapt to new navigation tasks while retaining previously learned knowledge.

title: CORE-Seg: Reasoning-Driven Segmentation for Complex Lesions via Reinforcement Learning
comment: Under Review with Computational Visual Media
summary: Medical image segmentation is undergoing a paradigm shift from conventional visual pattern matching to cognitive reasoning analysis. Although Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have shown promise in integrating linguistic and visual knowledge, significant gaps remain: existing general MLLMs possess broad common sense but lack the specialized visual reasoning required for complex lesions, whereas traditional segmentation models excel at pixel-level segmentation but lack logical interpretability. In this paper, we introduce ComLesion-14K, the first diverse Chain-of-Thought (CoT) benchmark for reasoning-driven complex lesion segmentation. To accomplish this task, we propose CORE-Seg, an end-to-end framework integrating reasoning with segmentation through a Semantic-Guided Prompt Adapter. We design a progressive training strategy from SFT to GRPO, equipped with an adaptive dual-granularity reward mechanism to mitigate reward sparsity. Our Method achieves state-of-the-art results with a mean Dice of 37.06\% (14.89\% higher than the second-best baseline), while reducing the failure rate to 18.42\%. Project Page: https://xyxl024.github.io/CORE-Seg.github.io/

AI.tldr: CORE-Seg is a reasoning-driven framework for complex lesion segmentation that integrates Chain-of-Thought reasoning with segmentation, achieving state-of-the-art performance with 37.06% mean Dice score.
AI.motivation: Medical image segmentation needs to shift from visual pattern matching to cognitive reasoning analysis. Existing MLLMs lack specialized visual reasoning for complex lesions, while traditional segmentation models lack logical interpretability.
AI.method: Proposes CORE-Seg, an end-to-end framework with Semantic-Guided Prompt Adapter, progressive training from SFT to GRPO, and adaptive dual-granularity reward mechanism to mitigate reward sparsity.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art results with mean Dice of 37.06% (14.89% higher than second-best baseline) and reduces failure rate to 18.42% on the ComLesion-14K benchmark.
AI.conclusion: The framework successfully integrates reasoning with segmentation for complex lesions, demonstrating significant improvements over existing methods and addressing the gap between general MLLMs and specialized segmentation models.

title: Agnostic learning in (almost) optimal time via Gaussian surface area
summary: The complexity of learning a concept class under Gaussian marginals in the difficult agnostic model is closely related to its $L_1$-approximability by low-degree polynomials. For any concept class with Gaussian surface area at most $Γ$, Klivans et al. (2008) show that degree $d = O(Γ^2 / \varepsilon^4)$ suffices to achieve an $\varepsilon$-approximation. This leads to the best-known bounds on the complexity of learning a variety of concept classes. In this note, we improve their analysis by showing that degree $d = \tilde O (Γ^2 / \varepsilon^2)$ is enough. In light of lower bounds due to Diakonikolas et al. (2021), this yields (near) optimal bounds on the complexity of agnostically learning polynomial threshold functions in the statistical query model. Our proof relies on a direct analogue of a construction of Feldman et al. (2020), who considered $L_1$-approximation on the Boolean hypercube.

AI.tldr: The paper improves the degree bound for L1-approximation of concept classes with bounded Gaussian surface area from O(Γ²/ε⁴) to Õ(Γ²/ε²), yielding near-optimal bounds for agnostically learning polynomial threshold functions in the statistical query model.
AI.motivation: Previous work by Klivans et al. (2008) established that degree d = O(Γ²/ε⁴) suffices for ε-approximation of concept classes with Gaussian surface area Γ. This note aims to improve this bound to achieve better complexity results for learning various concept classes, particularly in light of recent lower bounds by Diakonikolas et al. (2021).
AI.method: The proof uses a direct analogue of a construction by Feldman et al. (2020) for L1-approximation on the Boolean hypercube, adapting their approach to the Gaussian setting. The method focuses on improving the polynomial degree bound for approximating concept classes with bounded Gaussian surface area.
AI.result: The main result shows that degree d = Õ(Γ²/ε²) is sufficient for ε-approximation, improving the previous O(Γ²/ε⁴) bound. This yields near-optimal bounds on the complexity of agnostically learning polynomial threshold functions in the statistical query model.
AI.conclusion: The improved analysis provides tighter bounds on the complexity of learning concept classes under Gaussian marginals, achieving near-optimal results in light of known lower bounds. The construction adapts techniques from Boolean hypercube approximation to the Gaussian setting.

title: Multimodal Behavior Tree Generation: A Small Vision-Language Model for Robot Task Planning
summary: Large and small language models have been widely used for robotic task planning. At the same time, vision-language models (VLMs) have successfully tackled problems such as image captioning, scene understanding, and visual question answering. In this work, we combine these two approaches by deploying a compact, open-source multimodal model to generate behavior trees for robotic task planning. The main obstacle to achieving this goal is the lack of an existing dataset that links visual observations and instructions to executable behavior trees. We propose a method to construct such a dataset starting from existing robotic episodes (i.e., Open X-Embodiment), in which a large model serves as a teacher in a multi-stage generation pipeline. We use this dataset to fine-tune VLMs ranging from 500M to 4B parameters via parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT). The generated behavior trees, compatible with the BehaviorTree.CPP library, are evaluated both offline, using structural and lexical metrics, and online through the execution of household tasks in a state-of-the-art embodied simulator. Our results demonstrate that our fine-tuned 4B-parameter VLM approaches the performance of state-of-the-art closed-source models, achieving an 87\% success rate while requiring only a fraction of the computational resources.

AI.tldr: Fine-tuning vision-language models to generate executable behavior trees for robotic task planning from visual observations and instructions, achieving 87% success rate with 4B-parameter model.
AI.motivation: Combine language models for robotic planning with vision-language models' visual understanding capabilities. Need to address the lack of dataset linking visual observations/instructions to executable behavior trees.
AI.method: Construct dataset from existing robotic episodes (Open X-Embodiment) using large model as teacher in multi-stage generation pipeline. Fine-tune VLMs (500M-4B parameters) via parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) to generate BehaviorTree.CPP-compatible trees.
AI.result: Fine-tuned 4B-parameter VLM approaches performance of state-of-the-art closed-source models, achieving 87% success rate on household tasks in embodied simulator while requiring only fraction of computational resources.
AI.conclusion: Compact open-source multimodal models can effectively generate executable behavior trees for robotic task planning, bridging visual understanding with planning capabilities while being computationally efficient.

title: BlackMirror: Black-Box Backdoor Detection for Text-to-Image Models via Instruction-Response Deviation
comment: This paper is accepted by CVPR 2026
summary: This paper investigates the challenging task of detecting backdoored text-to-image models under black-box settings and introduces a novel detection framework BlackMirror. Existing approaches typically rely on analyzing image-level similarity, under the assumption that backdoor-triggered generations exhibit strong consistency across samples. However, they struggle to generalize to recently emerging backdoor attacks, where backdoored generations can appear visually diverse. BlackMirror is motivated by an observation: across backdoor attacks, {only partial semantic patterns within the generated image are steadily manipulated, while the rest of the content remains diverse or benign. Accordingly, BlackMirror consists of two components: MirrorMatch, which aligns visual patterns with the corresponding instructions to detect semantic deviations; and MirrorVerify, which evaluates the stability of these deviations across varied prompts to distinguish true backdoor behavior from benign responses. BlackMirror is a general, training-free framework that can be deployed as a plug-and-play module in Model-as-a-Service (MaaS) applications. Comprehensive experiments demonstrate that BlackMirror achieves accurate detection across a wide range of attacks. Code is available at https://github.com/Ferry-Li/BlackMirror.

AI.tldr: BlackMirror is a novel black-box detection framework for backdoored text-to-image models that identifies semantic pattern deviations rather than relying on image similarity, achieving accurate detection across diverse backdoor attacks.
AI.motivation: Existing detection methods fail against emerging backdoor attacks where backdoored generations appear visually diverse, as they rely on image-level similarity analysis assuming consistent outputs. The key insight is that only partial semantic patterns are steadily manipulated in backdoor attacks while other content remains diverse.
AI.method: BlackMirror consists of two components: MirrorMatch aligns visual patterns with instructions to detect semantic deviations, and MirrorVerify evaluates the stability of these deviations across varied prompts to distinguish true backdoor behavior from benign responses. It's a training-free, plug-and-play framework for MaaS applications.
AI.result: Comprehensive experiments demonstrate that BlackMirror achieves accurate detection across a wide range of backdoor attacks, outperforming existing approaches that struggle with visually diverse backdoored generations.
AI.conclusion: BlackMirror provides an effective solution for detecting backdoored text-to-image models in black-box settings by focusing on semantic pattern deviations rather than image similarity, making it generalizable to emerging attacks with diverse visual outputs.

title: Improved high-dimensional estimation with Langevin dynamics and stochastic weight averaging
summary: Significant recent work has studied the ability of gradient descent to recover a hidden planted direction $θ^\star \in S^{d-1}$ in different high-dimensional settings, including tensor PCA and single-index models. The key quantity that governs the ability of gradient descent to traverse these landscapes is the information exponent $k^\star$ (Ben Arous et al., (2021)), which corresponds to the order of the saddle at initialization in the population landscape. Ben Arous et al., (2021) showed that $n \gtrsim d^{\max(1, k^\star-1)}$ samples were necessary and sufficient for online SGD to recover $θ^\star$, and Ben Arous et al., (2020) proved a similar lower bound for Langevin dynamics. More recently, Damian et al., (2023) showed it was possible to circumvent these lower bounds by running gradient descent on a smoothed landscape, and that this algorithm succeeds with $n \gtrsim d^{\max(1, k^\star/2)}$ samples, which is optimal in the worst case. This raises the question of whether it is possible to achieve the same rate without explicit smoothing. In this paper, we show that Langevin dynamics can succeed with $n \gtrsim d^{ k^\star/2 }$ samples if one considers the average iterate, rather than the last iterate. The key idea is that the combination of noise-injection and iterate averaging is able to emulate the effect of landscape smoothing. We apply this result to both the tensor PCA and single-index model settings. Finally, we conjecture that minibatch SGD can also achieve the same rate without adding any additional noise.

AI.tldr: Langevin dynamics with iterate averaging achieves optimal sample complexity n ≳ d^{k*/2} for recovering planted directions in high-dimensional models, matching smoothed gradient descent performance without explicit smoothing.
AI.motivation: Previous work showed gradient descent requires n ≳ d^{max(1, k*-1)} samples to recover planted directions, while smoothed gradient descent achieves optimal n ≳ d^{max(1, k*/2)}. The paper investigates whether this optimal rate can be achieved without explicit smoothing.
AI.method: Uses Langevin dynamics (noise-injected gradient descent) with iterate averaging rather than last iterate. Shows that noise-injection combined with averaging emulates landscape smoothing effects.
AI.result: Proves Langevin dynamics with iterate averaging succeeds with n ≳ d^{k*/2} samples, achieving the same optimal rate as smoothed gradient descent. Applies results to tensor PCA and single-index models.
AI.conclusion: Noise-injection (Langevin dynamics) combined with iterate averaging can achieve optimal sample complexity without explicit smoothing. Conjectures minibatch SGD may also achieve same rate without additional noise.

title: Sticky-Glance: Robust Intent Recognition for Human Robot Collaboration via Single-Glance
summary: Gaze is a valuable means of communication for impaired people with extremely limited motor capabilities. However, robust gaze-based intent recognition in multi-object environments is challenging due to gaze noise, micro-saccades, viewpoint changes, and dynamic objects. To address this, we propose an object-centric gaze grounding framework that stabilizes intent through a sticky-glance algorithm, jointly modeling geometric distance and direction trends. The inferred intent remains anchored to the object even under short glances with minimal 3 gaze samples, achieving a tracking rate of 0.94 for dynamic targets and selection accuracy of 0.98 for static targets. We further introduce a continuous shared control and multi-modal interaction paradigm, enabling high-readiness control and human-in-loop feedback, thereby reducing task duration for nearly 10 \%. Experiments across dynamic tracking, multi-perspective alignment, a baseline comparison, user studies, and ablation studies demonstrate improved robustness, efficiency, and reduced workload compared to representative baselines.

AI.tldr: Object-centric gaze grounding framework with sticky-glance algorithm for robust intent recognition in multi-object environments, achieving high tracking and selection accuracy for impaired users.
AI.motivation: Gaze is valuable for impaired people with limited motor capabilities, but robust gaze-based intent recognition is challenging due to gaze noise, micro-saccades, viewpoint changes, and dynamic objects.
AI.method: Propose object-centric gaze grounding framework with sticky-glance algorithm that jointly models geometric distance and direction trends to stabilize intent, plus continuous shared control and multi-modal interaction paradigm.
AI.result: Achieves 0.94 tracking rate for dynamic targets and 0.98 selection accuracy for static targets with minimal 3 gaze samples, reduces task duration by nearly 10%, and demonstrates improved robustness, efficiency, and reduced workload.
AI.conclusion: The framework enables robust gaze-based intent recognition for impaired users in complex environments through object-centric grounding and sticky-glance stabilization, supporting high-readiness control with human-in-loop feedback.

title: RAC: Rectified Flow Auto Coder
comment: 11 Figures, 4 Tables. Project Page at https://world-snapshot.github.io/RAC/
summary: In this paper, we propose a Rectified Flow Auto Coder (RAC) inspired by Rectified Flow to replace the traditional VAE: 1. It achieves multi-step decoding by applying the decoder to flow timesteps. Its decoding path is straight and correctable, enabling step-by-step refinement. 2. The model inherently supports bidirectional inference, where the decoder serves as the encoder through time reversal (hence Coder rather than encoder or decoder), reducing parameter count by nearly 41%. 3. This generative decoding method improves generation quality since the model can correct latent variables along the path, partially addressing the reconstruction--generation gap. Experiments show that RAC surpasses SOTA VAEs in both reconstruction and generation with approximately 70% lower computational cost.

AI.tldr: RAC replaces VAE with rectified flow-based autoencoder, achieving straight/correctable decoding, bidirectional inference, and better quality with lower computation.
AI.motivation: To address limitations of traditional VAEs: non-straight decoding paths, separate encoder/decoder parameters, and the reconstruction-generation gap.
AI.method: Rectified Flow Auto Coder (RAC) uses flow timesteps for multi-step decoding, enables bidirectional inference (decoder serves as encoder via time reversal), and allows correction of latent variables along the path.
AI.result: RAC surpasses SOTA VAEs in both reconstruction and generation with ~70% lower computational cost and ~41% parameter reduction.
AI.conclusion: RAC provides a more efficient and effective alternative to VAEs with straight/correctable decoding, bidirectional inference, and improved generation quality.

title: Latent Diffusion-Based 3D Molecular Recovery from Vibrational Spectra
summary: Infrared (IR) spectroscopy, a type of vibrational spectroscopy, is widely used for molecular structure determination and provides critical structural information for chemists. However, existing approaches for recovering molecular structures from IR spectra typically rely on one-dimensional SMILES strings or two-dimensional molecular graphs, which fail to capture the intricate relationship between spectral features and three-dimensional molecular geometry. Recent advances in diffusion models have greatly enhanced the ability to generate molecular structures in 3D space. Yet, no existing model has explored the distribution of 3D molecular geometries corresponding to a single IR spectrum. In this work, we introduce IR-GeoDiff, a latent diffusion model that recovers 3D molecular geometries from IR spectra by integrating spectral information into both node and edge representations of molecular structures. We evaluate IR-GeoDiff from both spectral and structural perspectives, demonstrating its ability to recover the molecular distribution corresponding to a given IR spectrum. Furthermore, an attention-based analysis reveals that the model is able to focus on characteristic functional group regions in IR spectra, qualitatively consistent with common chemical interpretation practices.

AI.tldr: IR-GeoDiff: A latent diffusion model that generates 3D molecular geometries from infrared spectra by integrating spectral information into molecular node and edge representations.
AI.motivation: Existing methods for recovering molecular structures from IR spectra use 1D SMILES or 2D graphs, which fail to capture the relationship between spectral features and 3D molecular geometry. While diffusion models have improved 3D molecular generation, no model has explored the distribution of 3D geometries corresponding to a single IR spectrum.
AI.method: IR-GeoDiff is a latent diffusion model that recovers 3D molecular geometries from IR spectra by integrating spectral information into both node and edge representations of molecular structures. The model uses attention mechanisms to focus on characteristic functional group regions in spectra.
AI.result: The model successfully recovers molecular distributions corresponding to given IR spectra, evaluated from both spectral and structural perspectives. Attention-based analysis shows the model focuses on characteristic functional group regions in IR spectra, consistent with chemical interpretation practices.
AI.conclusion: IR-GeoDiff represents a significant advancement in molecular structure determination from IR spectra by enabling 3D geometry recovery, bridging the gap between spectral features and molecular spatial arrangements through diffusion modeling.

title: A Hazard-Informed Data Pipeline for Robotics Physical Safety
comment: 4th International Conference on Automation and Mechatronics Engineering (ICAME 2026)
summary: This report presents a structured Robotics Physical Safety Framework based on explicit asset declaration, systematic vulnerability enumeration, and hazard-driven synthetic data generation. The approach bridges classical risk engineering with modern machine learning pipelines, enabling safety envelope learning grounded in a formalized hazard ontology. The key contribution of this framework is the alignment between classical safety engineering, digital twin simulation, synthetic data generation, and machine learning model training.

AI.tldr: A robotics safety framework combining classical risk engineering with ML pipelines through asset declaration, vulnerability analysis, and synthetic data generation for safety envelope learning.
AI.motivation: To bridge the gap between traditional safety engineering approaches and modern machine learning methods in robotics, creating a more systematic and formalized approach to physical safety.
AI.method: Structured framework with explicit asset declaration, systematic vulnerability enumeration, hazard-driven synthetic data generation, and formalized hazard ontology for safety envelope learning.
AI.result: A framework that aligns classical safety engineering, digital twin simulation, synthetic data generation, and machine learning model training into a cohesive safety system.
AI.conclusion: The framework successfully integrates traditional risk engineering with modern ML pipelines to enable systematic robotics physical safety through formalized hazard analysis and synthetic data generation.

title: Towards Driver Behavior Understanding: Weakly-Supervised Risk Perception in Driving Scenes
