AI.result: Fly360 achieves stable omnidirectional obstacle avoidance and outperforms forward-view baselines across all three benchmark flight tasks in both simulation and real-world experiments.
AI.conclusion: The proposed Fly360 system successfully addresses the challenge of omnidirectional obstacle avoidance for panoramic drones, demonstrating superior performance over traditional forward-view approaches through comprehensive benchmark evaluation.

title: Adaptive Lipschitz-Free Conditional Gradient Methods for Stochastic Composite Nonconvex Optimization
summary: We propose ALFCG (Adaptive Lipschitz-Free Conditional Gradient), the first \textit{adaptive} projection-free framework for stochastic composite nonconvex minimization that \textit{requires neither global smoothness constants nor line search}. Unlike prior conditional gradient methods that use openloop diminishing stepsizes, conservative Lipschitz constants, or costly backtracking, ALFCG maintains a self-normalized accumulator of historical iterate differences to estimate local smoothness and minimize a quadratic surrogate model at each step. This retains the simplicity of Frank-Wolfe while adapting to unknown geometry. We study three variants. ALFCG-FS addresses finite-sum problems with a SPIDER estimator. ALFCG-MVR1 and ALFCG-MVR2 handle stochastic expectation problems by using momentum-based variance reduction with single-batch and two-batch updates, and operate under average and individual smoothness, respectively. To reach an $ε$-stationary point, ALFCG-FS attains $\mathcal{O}(N+\sqrt{N}ε^{-2})$ iteration complexity, while ALFCG-MVR1 and ALFCG-MVR2 achieve $\tilde{\mathcal{O}}(σ^2ε^{-4}+ε^{-2})$ and $\tilde{\mathcal{O}}(σε^{-3}+ε^{-2})$, where $N$ is the number of components and $σ$ is the noise level. In contrast to typical $\mathcal{O}(ε^{-4})$ or $\mathcal{O}(ε^{-3})$ rates, our bounds reduce to the optimal rate up to logarithmic factors $\tilde{\mathcal{O}}(ε^{-2})$ as the noise level $σ\to 0$. Extensive experiments on multiclass classification over nuclear norm balls and $\ell_p$ balls show that ALFCG generally outperforms state-of-the-art conditional gradient baselines.

AI.tldr: ALFCG is an adaptive projection-free method for stochastic composite nonconvex optimization that doesn't need global smoothness constants or line search, using historical iterate differences to estimate local smoothness.
AI.motivation: Existing conditional gradient methods require global smoothness constants, line search, or conservative Lipschitz constants, which can be impractical or computationally expensive for stochastic nonconvex problems.
AI.method: ALFCG uses a self-normalized accumulator of historical iterate differences to estimate local smoothness and minimizes a quadratic surrogate model at each step. Three variants: ALFCG-FS for finite-sum problems with SPIDER estimator, and ALFCG-MVR1/2 for stochastic expectation problems with momentum-based variance reduction.
AI.result: ALFCG-FS achieves O(N + √Nε⁻²) iteration complexity, while ALFCG-MVR1 and ALFCG-MVR2 achieve Õ(σ²ε⁻⁴ + ε⁻²) and Õ(σε⁻³ + ε⁻²) respectively. As noise level σ→0, rates reduce to optimal Õ(ε⁻²). Experiments show ALFCG outperforms state-of-the-art conditional gradient baselines.
AI.conclusion: ALFCG provides the first adaptive projection-free framework for stochastic composite nonconvex optimization that adapts to unknown geometry without requiring global smoothness constants or line search, achieving improved convergence rates.

title: VLM-RobustBench: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Robustness of Vision-Language Models
summary: Vision-language models (VLMs) achieve strong performance on standard, high-quality datasets, but we still do not fully understand how they perform under real-world image distortions. We present VLM-RobustBench, a benchmark spanning 49 augmentation types across noise, blur, weather, digital, and geometric perturbations, evaluated under graded severities (low/mid/high) and binary transforms, yielding 133 corrupted settings. We evaluate VLMs from four families (Qwen, InternVL, Molmo, Gemma) on two complementary benchmarks: MMBench (visually grounded) and MMMU-Pro (reasoning-oriented). Our results reveal that visual severity is a weak predictor of difficulty: low-severity spatial perturbations often degrade performance more than visually severe photometric corruptions. In particular, low-severity glass_blur reduces MMBench accuracy by about 8 pp on average across models, while the largest drops arise from resampling and geometric distortions (e.g., upsample, elastic_transform), reaching up to 34 pp. Overall, our findings suggest current VLMs are semantically strong but spatially fragile, motivating the definition of novel robustness evaluation protocols and training regimes that emphasize resampling and geometric invariances.

AI.tldr: VLM-RobustBench evaluates vision-language models under 49 real-world image distortions across 133 settings, revealing VLMs are semantically strong but spatially fragile, with geometric distortions causing largest performance drops.
AI.motivation: While VLMs perform well on standard datasets, their robustness to real-world image distortions is not well understood. The paper aims to systematically evaluate how VLMs handle various types of image corruptions encountered in practical applications.
AI.method: Created VLM-RobustBench with 49 augmentation types across noise, blur, weather, digital, and geometric perturbations, evaluated under graded severities (low/mid/high) and binary transforms (133 settings total). Evaluated VLMs from four families (Qwen, InternVL, Molmo, Gemma) on MMBench (visually grounded) and MMMU-Pro (reasoning-oriented) benchmarks.
AI.result: Visual severity is weak predictor of difficulty - low-severity spatial perturbations degrade performance more than visually severe photometric corruptions. Low-severity glass_blur reduces MMBench accuracy by ~8pp average. Largest drops from resampling and geometric distortions (upsample, elastic_transform) up to 34pp. Current VLMs are semantically strong but spatially fragile.
AI.conclusion: VLMs need improved robustness to spatial distortions. Findings motivate novel robustness evaluation protocols and training regimes emphasizing resampling and geometric invariances for more robust vision-language models.

title: RePer-360: Releasing Perspective Priors for 360$^\circ$ Depth Estimation via Self-Modulation
summary: Recent depth foundation models trained on perspective imagery achieve strong performance, yet generalize poorly to 360$^\circ$ images due to the substantial geometric discrepancy between perspective and panoramic domains. Moreover, fully fine-tuning these models typically requires large amounts of panoramic data. To address this issue, we propose RePer-360, a distortion-aware self-modulation framework for monocular panoramic depth estimation that adapts depth foundation models while preserving powerful pretrained perspective priors. Specifically, we design a lightweight geometry-aligned guidance module to derive a modulation signal from two complementary projections (i.e., ERP and CP) and use it to guide the model toward the panoramic domain without overwriting its pretrained perspective knowledge. We further introduce a Self-Conditioned AdaLN-Zero mechanism that produces pixel-wise scaling factors to reduce the feature distribution gap between the perspective and panoramic domains. In addition, a cubemap-domain consistency loss further improves training stability and cross-projection alignment. By shifting the focus from complementary-projection fusion to panoramic domain adaptation under preserved pretrained perspective priors, RePer-360 surpasses standard fine-tuning methods while using only 1\% of the training data. Under the same in-domain training setting, it further achieves an approximately 20\% improvement in RMSE. Code will be released upon acceptance.

AI.tldr: RePer-360 is a distortion-aware self-modulation framework that adapts depth foundation models to panoramic images while preserving pretrained perspective priors, achieving strong performance with minimal training data.
AI.motivation: Depth foundation models trained on perspective images generalize poorly to 360° panoramic images due to geometric discrepancies, and full fine-tuning requires large amounts of panoramic data.
AI.method: Uses a lightweight geometry-aligned guidance module with ERP and CP projections to generate modulation signals, Self-Conditioned AdaLN-Zero for pixel-wise scaling to reduce feature distribution gaps, and cubemap-domain consistency loss for training stability.
AI.result: Surpasses standard fine-tuning methods using only 1% of training data, achieves ~20% RMSE improvement under in-domain training settings, and effectively adapts to panoramic domain while preserving perspective priors.
AI.conclusion: RePer-360 successfully adapts depth foundation models to panoramic depth estimation with minimal data requirements by focusing on domain adaptation while preserving pretrained knowledge, offering an efficient solution for 360° depth estimation.

title: Adapter-Augmented Bandits for Online Multi-Constrained Multi-Modal Inference Scheduling
summary: Multi-modal large language model (MLLM) inference scheduling enables strong response quality under practical and heterogeneous budgets, beyond what a homogeneous single-backend setting can offer. Yet online MLLM task scheduling is nontrivial, as requests vary sharply in modality composition and latent reasoning difficulty, while execution backends incur distinct, time-varying costs due to system jitter and network variation. These coupled uncertainties pose two core challenges: deriving semantically faithful yet scheduling-relevant multi-modal task representations, and making low-overhead online decisions over irreversible multi-dimensional budgets. Accordingly, we propose \emph{M-CMAB} (\underline{M}ulti-modal \underline{M}ulti-constraint \underline{C}ontextual \underline{M}ulti-\underline{A}rmed \underline{B}andit), a multi-adapter-enhanced MLLM inference scheduling framework with three components: (i) a CLS-attentive, frozen-backbone \emph{Predictor} that extracts compact task representations and updates only lightweight adapters for action-specific estimation; (ii) a primal-dual \emph{Constrainer} that maintains online Lagrange multipliers to enforce long-horizon constraints via per-round objectives; and (iii) a two-phase \emph{Scheduler} that balances exploration and exploitation under irreversible budgets. We establish a regret guarantee under multi-dimensional knapsack constraints. On a composite multimodal benchmark with heterogeneous backends, \emph{M-CMAB} consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines across budget regimes, achieving up to 14.18% higher reward and closely tracking an oracle-aided upper bound. Codes are available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/M2CMAB/.

AI.tldr: M-CMAB is a multi-modal LLM inference scheduling framework that uses contextual bandits to optimize task allocation across heterogeneous backends under multi-dimensional budget constraints.
AI.motivation: Online MLLM task scheduling is challenging due to sharp variations in request modality composition and reasoning difficulty, coupled with time-varying backend costs from system jitter and network variation. Existing homogeneous single-backend approaches cannot achieve optimal response quality under practical heterogeneous budgets.
AI.method: Three-component framework: (1) CLS-attentive frozen-backbone Predictor extracts compact task representations using lightweight adapters; (2) primal-dual Constrainer maintains Lagrange multipliers for long-horizon constraint enforcement; (3) two-phase Scheduler balances exploration-exploitation under irreversible budgets using contextual multi-armed bandit approach.
AI.result: Achieves up to 14.18% higher reward than state-of-the-art baselines across budget regimes on composite multimodal benchmark with heterogeneous backends, closely tracking oracle-aided upper bound. Provides theoretical regret guarantee under multi-dimensional knapsack constraints.
AI.conclusion: M-CMAB enables effective MLLM inference scheduling under practical heterogeneous budgets, addressing coupled uncertainties in task characteristics and backend costs through a principled multi-constraint contextual bandit framework with theoretical guarantees.

title: Toward Generative Quantum Utility via Correlation-Complexity Map
summary: We propose a Correlation-Complexity Map as a practical diagnostic tool for determining when real-world data distributions are structurally aligned with IQP-type quantum generative models. Characterized by two complementary indicators: (i) a Quantum Correlation-Likeness Indicator (QCLI), computed from the dataset's correlation-order (Walsh-Hadamard/Fourier) power spectrum aggregated by interaction order and quantified via Jensen-Shannon divergence from an i.i.d. binomial reference; and (ii) a Classical Correlation-Complexity Indicator (CCI), defined as the fraction of total correlation not captured by the optimal Chow-Liu tree approximation, normalized by total correlation. We provide theoretical support by relating QCLI to a support-mismatch mechanism, for fixed-architecture IQP families trained with an MMD objective, higher QCLI implies a smaller irreducible approximation floor. Using the map, we identify the classical turbulence data as both IQP-compatible and classically complex (high QCLI/high CCI). Guided by this placement, we use an invertible float-to-bitstring representation and a latent-parameter adaptation scheme that reuses a compact IQP circuit over a temporal sequence by learning and interpolating a low-dimensional latent trajectory. In comparative evaluations against classical models such as Restricted Boltzmann Machine (RBM) and Deep Convolutional Generative Adversarial Networks (DCGAN), the IQP approach achieves competitive distributional alignment while using substantially fewer training snapshots and a small latent block, supporting the use of QCLI/CCI as practical indicators for locating IQP-aligned domains and advancing generative quantum utility.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a Correlation-Complexity Map with QCLI and CCI indicators to identify when real-world data aligns with IQP quantum models, validates it on turbulence data, and shows IQP models achieve competitive performance with fewer resources.
AI.motivation: To develop practical diagnostic tools for determining when real-world data distributions are structurally compatible with IQP-type quantum generative models, enabling efficient identification of quantum-advantage domains.
AI.method: Proposes a Correlation-Complexity Map with two indicators: QCLI (quantum correlation-likeness from Walsh-Hadamard power spectrum) and CCI (classical correlation-complexity from Chow-Liu tree approximation). Uses turbulence data with invertible float-to-bitstring representation and latent-parameter adaptation scheme for temporal sequences.
AI.result: Identifies classical turbulence data as both IQP-compatible and classically complex. IQP approach achieves competitive distributional alignment against RBM and DCGAN while using substantially fewer training snapshots and smaller latent blocks.
AI.conclusion: The QCLI/CCI indicators serve as practical tools for locating IQP-aligned domains, supporting generative quantum utility with resource-efficient quantum models that can compete with classical approaches.

title: Reflective Flow Sampling Enhancement
summary: The growing demand for text-to-image generation has led to rapid advances in generative modeling. Recently, text-to-image diffusion models trained with flow matching algorithms, such as FLUX, have achieved remarkable progress and emerged as strong alternatives to conventional diffusion models. At the same time, inference-time enhancement strategies have been shown to improve the generation quality and text-prompt alignment of text-to-image diffusion models. However, these techniques are mainly applicable to conventional diffusion models and usually fail to perform well on flow models. To bridge this gap, we propose Reflective Flow Sampling (RF-Sampling), a theoretically-grounded and training-free inference enhancement framework explicitly designed for flow models, especially for the CFG-distilled variants (i.e., models distilled from CFG guidance techniques), like FLUX. Departing from heuristic interpretations, we provide a formal derivation proving that RF-Sampling implicitly performs gradient ascent on the text-image alignment score. By leveraging a linear combination of textual representations and integrating them with flow inversion, RF-Sampling allows the model to explore noise spaces that are more consistent with the input prompt. Extensive experiments across multiple benchmarks demonstrate that RF-Sampling consistently improves both generation quality and prompt alignment. Moreover, RF-Sampling is also the first inference enhancement method that can exhibit test-time scaling ability to some extent on FLUX.

AI.tldr: RF-Sampling is a training-free inference enhancement framework for flow-based text-to-image models (like FLUX) that improves generation quality and prompt alignment through reflective sampling and flow inversion.
AI.motivation: Existing inference-time enhancement strategies work well for conventional diffusion models but fail on flow models, creating a gap that needs to be addressed for flow-based text-to-image generation systems.
AI.method: RF-Sampling uses a theoretically-grounded approach with formal derivation showing it performs gradient ascent on text-image alignment score. It combines textual representations with flow inversion to explore noise spaces more consistent with input prompts.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show RF-Sampling consistently improves generation quality and prompt alignment across multiple benchmarks, and is the first inference enhancement method to exhibit test-time scaling ability on FLUX.
AI.conclusion: RF-Sampling successfully bridges the gap for inference enhancement in flow models, providing a training-free solution that improves performance and shows promising scaling properties.

title: EffectMaker: Unifying Reasoning and Generation for Customized Visual Effect Creation
summary: Visual effects (VFX) are essential for enhancing the expressiveness and creativity of video content, yet producing high-quality effects typically requires expert knowledge and costly production pipelines. Existing AIGC systems face significant challenges in VFX generation due to the scarcity of effect-specific data and the inherent difficulty of modeling supernatural or stylized effects. Moreover, these approaches often require per-effect fine-tuning, which severely limits their scalability and generalization to novel VFX. In this work, we present EffectMaker, a unified reasoning-generation framework that enables reference-based VFX customization. EffectMaker employs a multimodal large language model to interpret high-level effect semantics and reason about how they should adapt to a target subject, while a diffusion transformer leverages in-context learning to capture fine-grained visual cues from reference videos. These two components form a semantic-visual dual-path guidance mechanism that enables accurate, controllable, and effect-consistent synthesis without per-effect fine-tuning. Furthermore, we construct EffectData, the largest high-quality synthetic dataset containing 130k videos across 3k VFX categories, to improve generalization and scalability. Experiments show that EffectMaker achieves superior visual quality and effect consistency over state-of-the-art baselines, offering a scalable and flexible paradigm for customized VFX generation. Project page: https://effectmaker.github.io

AI.tldr: EffectMaker is a unified reasoning-generation framework for reference-based visual effects customization that uses multimodal LLM reasoning and diffusion transformers for in-context learning, eliminating per-effect fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: Current VFX production requires expert knowledge and costly pipelines, while existing AIGC systems struggle with VFX generation due to data scarcity, difficulty modeling supernatural effects, and reliance on per-effect fine-tuning that limits scalability.
AI.method: A dual-path framework: 1) multimodal LLM interprets high-level effect semantics and reasons about adaptation to target subjects, 2) diffusion transformer uses in-context learning to capture fine-grained visual cues from reference videos, creating semantic-visual guidance without per-effect fine-tuning.
AI.result: EffectMaker achieves superior visual quality and effect consistency over SOTA baselines, enabled by EffectData - a 130k video dataset across 3k VFX categories that improves generalization and scalability.
AI.conclusion: EffectMaker offers a scalable, flexible paradigm for customized VFX generation that eliminates the need for per-effect fine-tuning while maintaining accurate, controllable, and effect-consistent synthesis.

title: NOBLE: Accelerating Transformers with Nonlinear Low-Rank Branches
summary: We introduce NOBLE (Nonlinear lOw-rank Branch for Linear Enhancement), an architectural augmentation that adds nonlinear low-rank branches to transformer linear layers. Unlike LoRA and other parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) methods, NOBLE is designed for pretraining from scratch. The branch is a permanent part of the architecture as opposed to an adapter for finetuning on top of frozen weights. The branch computes σ(xWdown)Wup where σ is a learnable nonlinearity. We evaluate several activation functions and find that CosNet, a two-layer cosine nonlinearity with learnable frequency and phase with a linear projection in between them in the bottleneck space, performs best. NOBLE achieves substantial improvements with minimal overhead: up to 1.47x step speedup to reach baseline eval loss (up to 32% fewer training steps), with as low as 4% additional parameters and 7% step time overhead, resulting in up to 1.22x net wallclock speedup. Experiments on LLMs (250M and 1.5B parameters), BERT, VQGAN, and ViT consistently show improved training efficiency. We identify one caveat: Mixup/CutMix augmentation interferes with NOBLE's benefits in Imagenet classification along with other stochastic augmentations, but when disabled, ViT also improves. This discrepancy is possibly explained by regularization techniques that encourage smoother fits to the target function while NOBLE may specialize more in sharper aspects of the target function.

AI.tldr: NOBLE adds nonlinear low-rank branches to transformer linear layers for pretraining, achieving faster convergence with minimal parameter overhead.
AI.motivation: Current PEFT methods like LoRA are designed for fine-tuning on frozen weights, but there's a need for architectural improvements that work during pretraining from scratch to improve training efficiency.
AI.method: NOBLE adds permanent nonlinear low-rank branches to transformer linear layers: σ(xWdown)Wup where σ is a learnable nonlinearity. Best performance with CosNet - a two-layer cosine nonlinearity with learnable frequency and phase.
AI.result: Achieves up to 1.47x step speedup (32% fewer training steps) with only 4% additional parameters and 7% step time overhead, resulting in up to 1.22x net wallclock speedup. Works across LLMs (250M-1.5B), BERT, VQGAN, and ViT.
AI.conclusion: NOBLE provides an effective architectural augmentation for pretraining transformers, improving training efficiency with minimal overhead, though may conflict with certain regularization techniques like Mixup/CutMix.

title: Contrastive-to-Self-Supervised: A Two-Stage Framework for Script Similarity Learning
summary: Learning similarity metrics for glyphs and writing systems faces a fundamental challenge: while individual graphemes within invented alphabets can be reliably labeled, the historical relationships between different scripts remain uncertain and contested. We propose a two-stage framework that addresses this epistemological constraint. First, we train an encoder with contrastive loss on labeled invented alphabets, establishing a teacher model with robust discriminative features. Second, we extend to historically attested scripts through teacher-student distillation, where the student learns unsupervised representations guided by the teacher's knowledge but free to discover latent cross-script similarities. The asymmetric setup enables the student to learn deformation-invariant embeddings while inheriting discriminative structure from clean examples. Our approach bridges supervised contrastive learning and unsupervised discovery, enabling both hard boundaries between distinct systems and soft similarities reflecting potential historical influences. Experiments on diverse writing systems demonstrate effective few-shot glyph recognition and meaningful script clustering without requiring ground-truth evolutionary relationships.

AI.tldr: Two-stage framework for learning glyph similarities: supervised contrastive learning on invented alphabets, then unsupervised teacher-student distillation on historical scripts to discover cross-script relationships without requiring ground-truth evolutionary labels.
AI.motivation: Learning similarity metrics for glyphs and writing systems is challenging because while individual graphemes in invented alphabets can be reliably labeled, historical relationships between different scripts remain uncertain and contested. There's a need to bridge supervised learning with unsupervised discovery of potential historical influences.
AI.method: Two-stage framework: 1) Train encoder with contrastive loss on labeled invented alphabets to create teacher model with robust discriminative features. 2) Extend to historically attested scripts through teacher-student distillation, where student learns unsupervised representations guided by teacher's knowledge but free to discover latent cross-script similarities. Asymmetric setup enables deformation-invariant embeddings while inheriting discriminative structure.
AI.result: Experiments on diverse writing systems demonstrate effective few-shot glyph recognition and meaningful script clustering without requiring ground-truth evolutionary relationships. The approach enables both hard boundaries between distinct systems and soft similarities reflecting potential historical influences.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework successfully bridges supervised contrastive learning and unsupervised discovery, addressing the epistemological constraint of uncertain historical relationships between scripts while enabling meaningful similarity learning and clustering of writing systems.

title: MOSIV: Multi-Object System Identification from Videos
summary: We introduce the challenging problem of multi-object system identification from videos, for which prior methods are ill-suited due to their focus on single-object scenes or discrete material classification with a fixed set of material prototypes. To address this, we propose MOSIV, a new framework that directly optimizes for continuous, per-object material parameters using a differentiable simulator guided by geometric objectives derived from video. We also present a new synthetic benchmark with contact-rich, multi-object interactions to facilitate evaluation. On this benchmark, MOSIV substantially improves grounding accuracy and long-horizon simulation fidelity over adapted baselines, establishing it as a strong baseline for this new task. Our analysis shows that object-level fine-grained supervision and geometry-aligned objectives are critical for stable optimization in these complex, multi-object settings. The source code and dataset will be released.

AI.tldr: MOSIV is a new framework for multi-object system identification from videos that optimizes continuous per-object material parameters using differentiable simulation guided by geometric objectives, outperforming adapted baselines on a new synthetic benchmark.
AI.motivation: Prior methods are ill-suited for multi-object system identification as they focus on single-object scenes or discrete material classification with fixed material prototypes, creating a gap for handling complex multi-object interactions.
AI.method: MOSIV directly optimizes continuous, per-object material parameters using a differentiable simulator guided by geometric objectives derived from video data, enabling fine-grained material estimation in multi-object settings.
AI.result: On a new synthetic benchmark with contact-rich, multi-object interactions, MOSIV substantially improves grounding accuracy and long-horizon simulation fidelity over adapted baselines, establishing it as a strong baseline for this new task.
AI.conclusion: Object-level fine-grained supervision and geometry-aligned objectives are critical for stable optimization in complex multi-object settings, and MOSIV provides an effective framework for multi-object system identification from videos.

title: COLD-Steer: Steering Large Language Models via In-Context One-step Learning Dynamics
summary: Activation steering methods enable inference-time control of large language model (LLM) behavior without retraining, but current approaches face a fundamental trade-off: sample-efficient methods suboptimally capture steering signals from labeled examples, while methods that better extract these signals require hundreds to thousands of examples. We introduce COLD-Steer, a training-free framework that steers LLM activations by approximating the representational changes that would result from gradient descent on in-context examples. Our key insight is that the effect of fine-tuning on a small set of examples can be efficiently approximated at inference time without actual parameter updates. We formalize this through two complementary approaches: (i) a unit kernel approximation method that updates the activations directly using gradients with respect to them, normalized across examples, and (ii) a finite-difference approximation requiring only two forward passes regardless of example count. Experiments across a variety of steering tasks and benchmarks demonstrate that COLD-Steer achieves upto 95% steering effectiveness while using 50 times fewer samples compared to the best baseline. COLD-Steer facilitates accommodating diverse perspectives without extensive demonstration data, which we validate through our experiments on pluralistic alignment tasks. Our framework opens new possibilities for adaptive, context-aware model control that can flexibly address varying loss-driven human preferences through principled approximation of learning dynamics rather than specialized training procedures.

AI.tldr: COLD-Steer is a training-free framework that steers LLM behavior by approximating gradient descent effects on activations using minimal examples, achieving 95% effectiveness with 50x fewer samples than baselines.
AI.motivation: Current activation steering methods face a trade-off: sample-efficient methods poorly capture steering signals, while effective methods require hundreds/thousands of examples. Need for inference-time control without extensive demonstration data.
AI.method: Approximates representational changes from gradient descent on in-context examples without actual parameter updates. Uses two approaches: (1) unit kernel approximation updating activations directly with normalized gradients, (2) finite-difference approximation requiring only two forward passes regardless of example count.
AI.result: Achieves up to 95% steering effectiveness while using 50 times fewer samples compared to best baselines. Validated across various steering tasks and benchmarks, including pluralistic alignment tasks.
AI.conclusion: COLD-Steer enables adaptive, context-aware model control without extensive data, opening new possibilities for flexible alignment with diverse human preferences through principled approximation of learning dynamics rather than specialized training.

title: Devil is in Narrow Policy: Unleashing Exploration in Driving VLA Models
summary: We identify a fundamental Narrow Policy limitation undermining the performance of autonomous VLA models, where driving Imitation Learning (IL) tends to collapse exploration and limit the potential of subsequent Reinforcement Learning (RL) stages, which often saturate prematurely due to insufficient feedback diversity. Thereby, we propose Curious-VLA, a framework that alleviates the exploit-explore dilemma through a two-stage design. During IL, we introduce a Feasible Trajectory Expansion (FTE) strategy to generate multiple physically valid trajectories and a step-wise normalized trajectory representation to adapt this diverse data. In the RL stage, we present Adaptive Diversity-Aware Sampling (ADAS) that prioritizes high-diversity samples and introduce Spanning Driving Reward (SDR) with a focal style weighting to amplify reward's value span for improving sensitivity to driving quality. On the Navsim benchmark, Curious-VLA achieves SoTA results (PDMS 90.3, EPDMS 85.4) and a Best-of-N PDMS of 94.8, demonstrating its effectiveness in unlocking the exploratory potential of VLA models. Code: https://github.com/Mashiroln/curious_vla.git.

AI.tldr: Curious-VLA addresses the narrow policy limitation in autonomous VLA models by introducing a two-stage framework that enhances exploration diversity in IL and improves RL feedback sensitivity, achieving state-of-the-art results on Navsim benchmark.
AI.motivation: The paper identifies a fundamental limitation in autonomous VLA models where driving Imitation Learning tends to collapse exploration, limiting subsequent Reinforcement Learning stages that saturate prematurely due to insufficient feedback diversity.
AI.method: Two-stage framework: 1) IL stage with Feasible Trajectory Expansion (FTE) to generate multiple physically valid trajectories and step-wise normalized trajectory representation; 2) RL stage with Adaptive Diversity-Aware Sampling (ADAS) prioritizing high-diversity samples and Spanning Driving Reward (SDR) with focal style weighting to amplify reward value span.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art results on Navsim benchmark: PDMS 90.3, EPDMS 85.4, and Best-of-N PDMS of 94.8, demonstrating effectiveness in unlocking exploratory potential of VLA models.
AI.conclusion: Curious-VLA successfully addresses the exploit-explore dilemma in autonomous VLA models through its two-stage design, enhancing exploration diversity and improving RL feedback sensitivity, leading to superior performance on driving benchmarks.

title: Cut to the Chase: Training-free Multimodal Summarization via Chain-of-Events
summary: Multimodal Summarization (MMS) aims to generate concise textual summaries by understanding and integrating information across videos, transcripts, and images. However, existing approaches still suffer from three main challenges: (1) reliance on domain-specific supervision, (2) implicit fusion with weak cross-modal grounding, and (3) flat temporal modeling without event transitions. To address these issues, we introduce **CoE**, a training-free MMS framework that performs structured reasoning through a **Chain-of-Events** guided by a Hierarchical Event Graph (HEG). The HEG encodes textual semantics into an explicit event hierarchy that scaffolds cross-modal grounding and temporal reasoning. Guided by this structure, **CoE** localizes key visual cues, models event evolution and causal transitions, and refines outputs via lightweight style adaptation for domain alignment. Extensive experiments on eight diverse datasets demonstrate that **CoE** consistently outperforms state-of-the-art video CoT baselines, achieving average gains of **+3.04 ROUGE**, **+9.51 CIDEr**, and **+1.88 BERTScore**, highlighting its robustness, interpretability, and cross-domain generalization. Our code is available at https://github.com/youxiaoxing/CoE.

AI.tldr: CoE is a training-free multimodal summarization framework that uses Chain-of-Events reasoning guided by a Hierarchical Event Graph to address challenges in cross-modal grounding and temporal modeling.
AI.motivation: Existing multimodal summarization approaches suffer from three main challenges: reliance on domain-specific supervision, implicit fusion with weak cross-modal grounding, and flat temporal modeling without event transitions.
AI.method: CoE performs structured reasoning through a Chain-of-Events guided by a Hierarchical Event Graph (HEG). The HEG encodes textual semantics into an explicit event hierarchy that scaffolds cross-modal grounding and temporal reasoning. The framework localizes key visual cues, models event evolution and causal transitions, and refines outputs via lightweight style adaptation.
AI.result: CoE consistently outperforms state-of-the-art video CoT baselines across eight diverse datasets, achieving average gains of +3.04 ROUGE, +9.51 CIDEr, and +1.88 BERTScore.
AI.conclusion: CoE demonstrates robustness, interpretability, and cross-domain generalization in multimodal summarization through its training-free, structured reasoning approach with explicit event hierarchy modeling.

title: StruVis: Enhancing Reasoning-based Text-to-Image Generation via Thinking with Structured Vision
summary: Reasoning-based text-to-image (T2I) generation requires models to interpret complex prompts accurately. Existing reasoning frameworks can be broadly categorized into two types: (1) Text-Only Reasoning, which is computationally efficient but lacks access to visual context, often resulting in the omission of critical spatial and visual elements; and (2) Text-Image Interleaved Reasoning, which leverages a T2I generator to provide visual references during the reasoning process. While this approach enhances visual grounding, it incurs substantial computational costs and constrains the reasoning capacity of MLLMs to the representational limitations of the generator. To this end, we propose StruVis, a novel framework that enhances T2I generation through Thinking with Structured Vision. Instead of relying on intermediate image generation, StruVis employs text-based structured visual representations as intermediate reasoning states, thereby enabling the MLLM to effectively "perceive" visual structure within a purely text-based reasoning process. Powered by this, the reasoning potential for T2I generation of the MLLM is unlocked through structured-vision-guided reasoning. Additionally, as a generator-agnostic reasoning framework, our proposed StruVis can be seamlessly integrated with diverse T2I generators and efficiently enhance their performance in reasoning-based T2I generation. Extensive experiments demonstrate that StruVis achieves significant performance improvements on reasoning-based T2I benchmarks, e.g., a 4.61% gain on T2I-ReasonBench and a 4% gain on WISE.

AI.tldr: StruVis is a novel framework that enhances text-to-image generation by using text-based structured visual representations instead of intermediate image generation, enabling more efficient and effective reasoning without computational overhead.
AI.motivation: Existing reasoning frameworks for text-to-image generation have limitations: text-only reasoning lacks visual context and misses spatial elements, while text-image interleaved reasoning is computationally expensive and constrained by generator limitations. There's a need for a more efficient approach that maintains visual grounding without computational overhead.
AI.method: StruVis employs text-based structured visual representations as intermediate reasoning states, allowing MLLMs to "perceive" visual structure within a purely text-based reasoning process. It's a generator-agnostic framework that uses structured-vision-guided reasoning to unlock MLLM's reasoning potential for T2I generation.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show significant performance improvements: 4.61% gain on T2I-ReasonBench and 4% gain on WISE benchmarks. The framework can be seamlessly integrated with diverse T2I generators to efficiently enhance their performance.
AI.conclusion: StruVis provides an effective solution to the limitations of existing reasoning frameworks by using structured visual representations in text form, achieving better performance while being computationally efficient and generator-agnostic.

title: When One Modality Rules Them All: Backdoor Modality Collapse in Multimodal Diffusion Models
comment: Accepted to the ICLR 2026 Workshop on Principled Design for Trustworthy AI. The first two authors contributed equally
summary: While diffusion models have revolutionized visual content generation, their rapid adoption has underscored the critical need to investigate vulnerabilities, e.g., to backdoor attacks. In multimodal diffusion models, it is natural to expect that attacking multiple modalities simultaneously (e.g., text and image) would yield complementary effects and strengthen the overall backdoor. In this paper, we challenge this assumption by investigating the phenomenon of Backdoor Modality Collapse, a scenario where the backdoor mechanism degenerates to rely predominantly on a subset of modalities, rendering others redundant. To rigorously quantify this behavior, we introduce two novel metrics: Trigger Modality Attribution (TMA) and Cross-Trigger Interaction (CTI). Through extensive experiments across diverse training configurations in multimodal conditional diffusion, we consistently observe a ``winner-takes-all'' dynamic in backdoor behavior. Our results reveal that (1) attacks often collapse into subset-modality dominance, and (2) cross-modal interaction is negligible or even negative, contradicting the intuition of synergistic vulnerability. These findings highlight a critical blind spot in current assessments, suggesting that high attack success rates often mask a fundamental reliance on a subset of modalities. This establishes a principled foundation for mechanistic analysis and future defense development.

AI.tldr: The paper discovers "Backdoor Modality Collapse" in multimodal diffusion models, where backdoor attacks degenerate to rely on only a subset of modalities despite attacking multiple modalities simultaneously, challenging the assumption of complementary effects.
AI.motivation: While diffusion models have revolutionized visual content generation, their rapid adoption has created critical vulnerabilities to backdoor attacks. The natural assumption is that attacking multiple modalities simultaneously (e.g., text and image) would yield complementary effects and strengthen backdoors, but this paper challenges that assumption.
AI.method: The authors introduce two novel metrics to quantify backdoor modality collapse: Trigger Modality Attribution (TMA) and Cross-Trigger Interaction (CTI). They conduct extensive experiments across diverse training configurations in multimodal conditional diffusion models to analyze backdoor behavior.
AI.result: The experiments consistently reveal a "winner-takes-all" dynamic where backdoor attacks collapse into subset-modality dominance. Cross-modal interaction is found to be negligible or even negative, contradicting the intuition of synergistic vulnerability. High attack success rates often mask fundamental reliance on a subset of modalities.
AI.conclusion: The findings highlight a critical blind spot in current security assessments of multimodal diffusion models. The discovery of Backdoor Modality Collapse establishes a principled foundation for mechanistic analysis and future defense development against backdoor attacks.

title: Transforming Omnidirectional RGB-LiDAR data into 3D Gaussian Splatting
comment: This work has been submitted to the 2026 IEEE/RSJ International Conference on Intelligent Robots and Systems (IROS) for possible publication
summary: The demand for large-scale digital twins is rapidly growing in robotics and autonomous driving. However, constructing these environments with 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) usually requires expensive, purpose-built data collection. Meanwhile, deployed platforms routinely collect extensive omnidirectional RGB and LiDAR logs, but a significant portion of these sensor data is directly discarded or strictly underutilized due to transmission constraints and the lack of scalable reuse pipeline. In this paper, we present an omnidirectional RGB-LiDAR reuse pipeline that transforms these archived logs into robust initialization assets for 3DGS. Direct conversion of such raw logs introduces practical bottlenecks: inherent non-linear distortion leads to unreliable Structure-from-Motion (SfM) tracking, and dense, unorganized LiDAR clouds cause computational overhead during 3DGS optimization. To overcome these challenges, our pipeline strategically integrates an ERP-to-cubemap conversion module for deterministic spatial anchoring, alongside PRISM-a color stratified downsampling strategy. By bridging these multi-modal inputs via Fast Point Feature Histograms (FPFH) based global registration and Iterative Closest Point (ICP), our pipeline successfully repurposes a considerable fraction of discarded data into usable SfM geometry. Furthermore, our LiDAR-reinforced initialization consistently enhances the final 3DGS rendering fidelity in structurally complex scenes compared to vision-only baselines. Ultimately, this work provides a deterministic workflow for creating simulation-grade digital twins from standard archived sensor logs.

AI.tldr: A pipeline to repurpose archived omnidirectional RGB-LiDAR logs into 3D Gaussian Splatting initialization assets for digital twins, overcoming distortion and computational bottlenecks.
AI.motivation: Large-scale digital twins require expensive data collection, while deployed platforms routinely collect extensive RGB-LiDAR logs that are discarded due to transmission constraints and lack of scalable reuse pipelines.
AI.method: Omnidirectional RGB-LiDAR reuse pipeline with ERP-to-cubemap conversion for spatial anchoring, PRISM color stratified downsampling, FPFH-based global registration, and ICP to bridge multi-modal inputs.
AI.result: Pipeline successfully repurposes discarded data into usable SfM geometry and enhances 3DGS rendering fidelity in complex scenes compared to vision-only baselines.
AI.conclusion: Provides a deterministic workflow for creating simulation-grade digital twins from standard archived sensor logs, enabling scalable reuse of underutilized data.

title: TaPD: Temporal-adaptive Progressive Distillation for Observation-Adaptive Trajectory Forecasting in Autonomous Driving
summary: Trajectory prediction is essential for autonomous driving, enabling vehicles to anticipate the motion of surrounding agents to support safe planning. However, most existing predictors assume fixed-length histories and suffer substantial performance degradation when observations are variable or extremely short in real-world settings (e.g., due to occlusion or a limited sensing range). We propose TaPD (Temporal-adaptive Progressive Distillation), a unified plug-and-play framework for observation-adaptive trajectory forecasting under variable history lengths. TaPD comprises two cooperative modules: an Observation-Adaptive Forecaster (OAF) for future prediction and a Temporal Backfilling Module (TBM) for explicit reconstruction of the past. OAF is built on progressive knowledge distillation (PKD), which transfers motion pattern knowledge from long-horizon "teachers" to short-horizon "students" via hierarchical feature regression, enabling short observations to recover richer motion context. We further introduce a cosine-annealed distillation weighting scheme to balance forecasting supervision and feature alignment, improving optimization stability and cross-length consistency. For extremely short histories where implicit alignment is insufficient, TBM backfills missing historical segments conditioned on scene evolution, producing context-rich trajectories that strengthen PKD and thereby improve OAF. We employ a decoupled pretrain-reconstruct-finetune protocol to preserve real-motion priors while adapting to backfilled inputs. Extensive experiments on Argoverse 1 and Argoverse 2 show that TaPD consistently outperforms strong baselines across all observation lengths, delivers especially large gains under very short inputs, and improves other predictors (e.g., HiVT) in a plug-and-play manner. Code will be available at https://github.com/zhouhao94/TaPD.

AI.tldr: TaPD is a plug-and-play framework for trajectory prediction that handles variable observation lengths through progressive knowledge distillation and temporal backfilling.
AI.motivation: Existing trajectory predictors assume fixed-length histories and degrade with variable or extremely short observations in real-world scenarios (occlusions, limited sensing range).
AI.method: Two cooperative modules: 1) Observation-Adaptive Forecaster (OAF) using progressive knowledge distillation to transfer motion patterns from long to short observations, 2) Temporal Backfilling Module (TBM) that reconstructs missing historical segments. Uses cosine-annealed distillation weighting and decoupled pretrain-reconstruct-finetune protocol.
AI.result: Outperforms strong baselines across all observation lengths on Argoverse 1 and 2, especially large gains with very short inputs. Works as plug-and-play enhancement for other predictors like HiVT.
AI.conclusion: TaPD provides a unified solution for observation-adaptive trajectory forecasting that handles variable history lengths effectively, improving real-world applicability where observations are often incomplete.

title: Occlusion-Aware SORT: Observing Occlusion for Robust Multi-Object Tracking
comment: The IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition 2026 (CVPR2026)
summary: Multi-object tracking (MOT) involves analyzing object trajectories and counting the number of objects in video sequences. However, 2D MOT faces challenges due to positional cost confusion arising from partial occlusion. To address this issue, we present the novel Occlusion-Aware SORT (OA-SORT) framework, a plug-and-play and training-free framework that includes the Occlusion-Aware Module (OAM), the Occlusion-Aware Offset (OAO), and the Bias-Aware Momentum (BAM). Specifically, OAM analyzes the occlusion status of objects, where a Gaussian Map (GM) is introduced to reduce background influence. In contrast, OAO and BAM leverage the OAM-described occlusion status to mitigate cost confusion and suppress estimation instability. Comprehensive evaluations on the DanceTrack, SportsMOT, and MOT17 datasets demonstrate the importance of occlusion handling in MOT. On the DanceTrack test set, OA-SORT achieves 63.1% and 64.2% in HOTA and IDF1, respectively. Furthermore, integrating the Occlusion-Aware framework into the four additional trackers improves HOTA and IDF1 by an average of 2.08% and 3.05%, demonstrating the reusability of the occlusion awareness.

AI.tldr: OA-SORT is a plug-and-play, training-free multi-object tracking framework that addresses occlusion challenges through three novel components: Occlusion-Aware Module, Occlusion-Aware Offset, and Bias-Aware Momentum.
AI.motivation: 2D multi-object tracking faces challenges from positional cost confusion caused by partial occlusion, which degrades tracking performance and object counting accuracy in video sequences.
AI.method: Proposes OA-SORT framework with three components: 1) Occlusion-Aware Module (OAM) analyzes occlusion status using Gaussian Map to reduce background influence, 2) Occlusion-Aware Offset (OAO) mitigates cost confusion, and 3) Bias-Aware Momentum (BAM) suppresses estimation instability.
AI.result: Achieves 63.1% HOTA and 64.2% IDF1 on DanceTrack test set. When integrated into four additional trackers, improves HOTA and IDF1 by average of 2.08% and 3.05% respectively, demonstrating strong reusability.
AI.conclusion: The occlusion-aware framework effectively addresses occlusion challenges in MOT, showing significant performance improvements and demonstrating the importance of occlusion handling through plug-and-play, training-free components.

title: Hierarchical Industrial Demand Forecasting with Temporal and Uncertainty Explanations
summary: Hierarchical time-series forecasting is essential for demand prediction across various industries. While machine learning models have obtained significant accuracy and scalability on such forecasting tasks, the interpretability of their predictions, informed by application, is still largely unexplored. To bridge this gap, we introduce a novel interpretability method for large hierarchical probabilistic time-series forecasting, adapting generic interpretability techniques while addressing challenges associated with hierarchical structures and uncertainty. Our approach offers valuable interpretative insights in response to real-world industrial supply chain scenarios, including 1) the significance of various time-series within the hierarchy and external variables at specific time points, 2) the impact of different variables on forecast uncertainty, and 3) explanations for forecast changes in response to modifications in the training dataset. To evaluate the explainability method, we generate semi-synthetic datasets based on real-world scenarios of explaining hierarchical demands for over ten thousand products at a large chemical company. The experiments showed that our explainability method successfully explained state-of-the-art industrial forecasting methods with significantly higher explainability accuracy. Furthermore, we provide multiple real-world case studies that show the efficacy of our approach in identifying important patterns and explanations that help stakeholders better understand the forecasts. Additionally, our method facilitates the identification of key drivers behind forecasted demand, enabling more informed decision-making and strategic planning. Our approach helps build trust and confidence among users, ultimately leading to better adoption and utilization of hierarchical forecasting models in practice.

AI.tldr: Novel interpretability method for hierarchical probabilistic time-series forecasting that provides explanations for industrial supply chain scenarios, addressing hierarchical structures and uncertainty while improving explainability accuracy.
AI.motivation: While machine learning models have achieved good accuracy in hierarchical time-series forecasting for demand prediction, their predictions lack interpretability which is crucial for real-world industrial applications like supply chain management.
AI.method: Adapts generic interpretability techniques to address hierarchical structures and uncertainty in probabilistic forecasting. The method explains: 1) significance of time-series within hierarchy and external variables at specific times, 2) impact of variables on forecast uncertainty, and 3) forecast changes due to training data modifications.
AI.result: Evaluated on semi-synthetic datasets based on real-world hierarchical demands for over 10,000 products at a chemical company. The method successfully explained state-of-the-art industrial forecasting methods with significantly higher explainability accuracy. Real-world case studies showed efficacy in identifying important patterns and explanations.
AI.conclusion: The interpretability method helps build trust and confidence among users, enables better understanding of forecasts, facilitates identification of key demand drivers, and leads to better adoption/utilization of hierarchical forecasting models in practice.

title: VG3S: Visual Geometry Grounded Gaussian Splatting for Semantic Occupancy Prediction
summary: 3D semantic occupancy prediction has become a crucial perception task for comprehensive scene understanding in autonomous driving. While recent advances have explored 3D Gaussian splatting for occupancy modeling to substantially reduce computational overhead, the generation of high-quality 3D Gaussians relies heavily on accurate geometric cues, which are often insufficient in purely vision-centric paradigms. To bridge this gap, we advocate for injecting the strong geometric grounding capability from Vision Foundation Models (VFMs) into occupancy prediction. In this regard, we introduce Visual Geometry Grounded Gaussian Splatting (VG3S), a novel framework that empowers Gaussian-based occupancy prediction with cross-view 3D geometric grounding. Specifically, to fully exploit the rich 3D geometric priors from a frozen VFM, we propose a plug-and-play hierarchical geometric feature adapter, which can effectively transform generic VFM tokens via feature aggregation, task-specific alignment, and multi-scale restructuring. Extensive experiments on the nuScenes occupancy benchmark demonstrate that VG3S achieves remarkable improvements of 12.6% in IoU and 7.5% in mIoU over the baseline. Furthermore, we show that VG3S generalizes seamlessly across diverse VFMs, consistently enhancing occupancy prediction accuracy and firmly underscoring the immense value of integrating priors derived from powerful, pre-trained geometry-grounded VFMs.

AI.tldr: VG3S improves 3D semantic occupancy prediction by injecting geometric priors from Vision Foundation Models into Gaussian splatting, achieving significant performance gains on nuScenes benchmark.
AI.motivation: Current 3D Gaussian splatting for occupancy prediction relies on accurate geometric cues that are insufficient in vision-only systems. There's a need to leverage the strong geometric grounding capabilities of Vision Foundation Models to improve occupancy prediction quality.
AI.method: Proposes Visual Geometry Grounded Gaussian Splatting (VG3S) framework with a plug-and-play hierarchical geometric feature adapter. This adapter transforms generic VFM tokens through feature aggregation, task-specific alignment, and multi-scale restructuring to inject 3D geometric priors into Gaussian-based occupancy prediction.
AI.result: VG3S achieves remarkable improvements of 12.6% in IoU and 7.5% in mIoU over baseline on nuScenes occupancy benchmark. The framework generalizes well across diverse VFMs, consistently enhancing occupancy prediction accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Integrating priors from pre-trained geometry-grounded Vision Foundation Models provides immense value for 3D semantic occupancy prediction, demonstrating the effectiveness of VG3S framework in bridging the geometric gap in vision-centric paradigms.

title: GazeMoE: Perception of Gaze Target with Mixture-of-Experts
summary: Estimating human gaze target from visible images is a critical task for robots to understand human attention, yet the development of generalizable neural architectures and training paradigms remains challenging. While recent advances in pre-trained vision foundation models offer promising avenues for locating gaze targets, the integration of multi-modal cues -- including eyes, head poses, gestures, and contextual features -- demands adaptive and efficient decoding mechanisms. Inspired by Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) for adaptive domain expertise in large vision-language models, we propose GazeMoE, a novel end-to-end framework that selectively leverages gaze-target-related cues from a frozen foundation model through MoE modules. To address class imbalance in gaze target classification (in-frame vs. out-of-frame) and enhance robustness, GazeMoE incorporates a class-balancing auxiliary loss alongside strategic data augmentations, including region-specific cropping and photometric transformations. Extensive experiments on benchmark datasets demonstrate that our GazeMoE achieves state-of-the-art performance, outperforming existing methods on challenging gaze estimation tasks. The code and pre-trained models are released at https://huggingface.co/zdai257/GazeMoE

AI.tldr: GazeMoE: A novel end-to-end framework using Mixture-of-Experts modules to selectively leverage gaze-target-related cues from frozen foundation models, achieving state-of-the-art performance on gaze estimation tasks.
AI.motivation: Estimating human gaze targets from visible images is critical for robots to understand human attention, but developing generalizable neural architectures and training paradigms remains challenging. While pre-trained vision foundation models offer promising avenues, integrating multi-modal cues (eyes, head poses, gestures, context) requires adaptive and efficient decoding mechanisms.
AI.method: Proposes GazeMoE, an end-to-end framework inspired by Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) that selectively leverages gaze-target-related cues from a frozen foundation model through MoE modules. Incorporates class-balancing auxiliary loss to address class imbalance (in-frame vs. out-of-frame) and uses strategic data augmentations including region-specific cropping and photometric transformations.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on benchmark datasets demonstrate that GazeMoE achieves state-of-the-art performance, outperforming existing methods on challenging gaze estimation tasks.
AI.conclusion: GazeMoE effectively addresses gaze estimation challenges by adaptively integrating multi-modal cues through MoE modules while handling class imbalance and enhancing robustness through strategic techniques. The framework shows superior performance and the code/models are publicly available.

title: Ensemble Learning with Sparse Hypercolumns
comment: presented at 33rd International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Cognitive Science (AICS 2025)
summary: Directly inspired by findings in biological vision, high-dimensional hypercolumns are feature vectors built by concatenating multi-scale activations of convolutional neural networks for a single image pixel location. Together with powerful classifiers, they can be used for image segmentation i.e. pixel classification. However, in practice, there are only very few works dedicated to the use of hypercolumns. One reason is the computational complexity of processing concatenated dense hypercolumns that grows linearly with the size $N$ of the training set. In this work, we address this challenge by applying stratified subsampling to the VGG16 based hypercolumns. Furthermore, we investigate the performance of ensemble learning on sparse hypercolumns. Our experiments on a brain tumor dataset show that stacking and voting ensembles deliver competitive performance, but in the extreme low-shot case of $N \leq 20$, a simple Logistic Regression classifier is the most effective method. For 10% stratified subsampling rate, our best average Dice score is 0.66 for $N=20$. This is a statistically significant improvement of 24.53% over the standard multi-scale UNet baseline ($p$-value = $[3.07e-11]$, Wilcoxon signed-rank test), which is less effective due to overfitting.

AI.tldr: Hypercolumns with stratified subsampling and ensemble learning achieve 24.53% improvement over UNet baseline for brain tumor segmentation, especially effective in low-shot learning scenarios (N≤20).
AI.motivation: Hypercolumns are biologically-inspired feature vectors for pixel classification, but their computational complexity grows linearly with training set size, limiting practical use. The paper aims to address this computational challenge.
AI.method: Applied stratified subsampling to VGG16-based hypercolumns to reduce computational complexity. Investigated ensemble learning (stacking and voting) on sparse hypercolumns. Compared with Logistic Regression for extreme low-shot cases.
AI.result: For 10% stratified subsampling rate with N=20, best average Dice score is 0.66. This represents a statistically significant 24.53% improvement over standard multi-scale UNet baseline (p-value = 3.07e-11). In extreme low-shot cases (N≤20), simple Logistic Regression outperforms ensemble methods.
AI.conclusion: Stratified subsampling of hypercolumns combined with appropriate classifier selection (ensembles for moderate data, Logistic Regression for extreme low-shot) provides effective brain tumor segmentation with significant improvements over baseline methods while addressing computational complexity.

title: Causal Interpretation of Neural Network Computations with Contribution Decomposition
summary: Understanding how neural networks transform inputs into outputs is crucial for interpreting and manipulating their behavior. Most existing approaches analyze internal representations by identifying hidden-layer activation patterns correlated with human-interpretable concepts. Here we take a direct approach to examine how hidden neurons act to drive network outputs. We introduce CODEC (Contribution Decomposition), a method that uses sparse autoencoders to decompose network behavior into sparse motifs of hidden-neuron contributions, revealing causal processes that cannot be determined by analyzing activations alone. Applying CODEC to benchmark image-classification networks, we find that contributions grow in sparsity and dimensionality across layers and, unexpectedly, that they progressively decorrelate positive and negative effects on network outputs. We further show that decomposing contributions into sparse modes enables greater control and interpretation of intermediate layers, supporting both causal manipulations of network output and human-interpretable visualizations of distinct image components that combine to drive that output. Finally, by analyzing state-of-the-art models of neural activity in the vertebrate retina, we demonstrate that CODEC uncovers combinatorial actions of model interneurons and identifies the sources of dynamic receptive fields. Overall, CODEC provides a rich and interpretable framework for understanding how nonlinear computations evolve across hierarchical layers, establishing contribution modes as an informative unit of analysis for mechanistic insights into artificial neural networks.

AI.tldr: CODEC decomposes neural network behavior into sparse motifs of hidden-neuron contributions, revealing causal processes that activation analysis alone cannot determine.
AI.motivation: Existing approaches analyze neural networks by identifying activation patterns correlated with human concepts, but this doesn't reveal how neurons actually drive outputs. There's a need for direct examination of how hidden neurons causally contribute to network behavior.
AI.method: CODEC (Contribution Decomposition) uses sparse autoencoders to decompose network behavior into sparse motifs of hidden-neuron contributions, revealing causal processes that cannot be determined by analyzing activations alone.
AI.result: Applied to image-classification networks, contributions grow in sparsity and dimensionality across layers, and unexpectedly decorrelate positive/negative effects on outputs. CODEC enables greater control and interpretation of intermediate layers, supporting causal manipulations and human-interpretable visualizations. In retina models, it uncovers combinatorial interneuron actions and identifies dynamic receptive field sources.
AI.conclusion: CODEC provides a rich, interpretable framework for understanding nonlinear computations across hierarchical layers, establishing contribution modes as an informative unit of analysis for mechanistic insights into artificial neural networks.

title: FontUse: A Data-Centric Approach to Style- and Use-Case-Conditioned In-Image Typography
summary: Recent text-to-image models can generate high-quality images from natural-language prompts, yet controlling typography remains challenging: requested typographic appearance is often ignored or only weakly followed. We address this limitation with a data-centric approach that trains image generation models using targeted supervision derived from a structured annotation pipeline specialized for typography. Our pipeline constructs a large-scale typography-focused dataset, FontUse, consisting of about 70K images annotated with user-friendly prompts, text-region locations, and OCR-recognized strings. The annotations are automatically produced using segmentation models and multimodal large language models (MLLMs). The prompts explicitly combine font styles (e.g., serif, script, elegant) and use cases (e.g., wedding invitations, coffee-shop menus), enabling intuitive specification even for novice users. Fine-tuning existing generators with these annotations allows them to consistently interpret style and use-case conditions as textual prompts without architectural modification. For evaluation, we introduce a Long-CLIP-based metric that measures alignment between generated typography and requested attributes. Experiments across diverse prompts and layouts show that models trained with our pipeline produce text renderings more consistent with prompts than competitive baselines. The source code for our annotation pipeline is available at https://github.com/xiaxinz/FontUSE.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces FontUse, a data-centric approach to improve typography control in text-to-image models by creating a large-scale typography-focused dataset with structured annotations and fine-tuning existing generators.
AI.motivation: Current text-to-image models struggle with controlling typography - requested typographic appearance is often ignored or weakly followed, limiting their practical applications where precise typography matters.
AI.method: 1) Created FontUse dataset (~70K images) with automated annotations using segmentation models and MLLMs; 2) Structured prompts combining font styles and use cases; 3) Fine-tuned existing image generators with these annotations; 4) Introduced Long-CLIP-based metric for evaluation.
AI.result: Models trained with FontUse pipeline produce text renderings more consistent with prompts than competitive baselines across diverse prompts and layouts, achieving better typography control without architectural modifications.
AI.conclusion: The data-centric approach with structured typography annotations enables text-to-image models to better interpret style and use-case conditions, providing intuitive typography control for users while maintaining model architecture compatibility.

title: A recipe for scalable attention-based MLIPs: unlocking long-range accuracy with all-to-all node attention
summary: Machine-learning interatomic potentials (MLIPs) have advanced rapidly, with many top models relying on strong physics-based inductive biases. However, as models scale to larger systems like biomolecules and electrolytes, they struggle to accurately capture long-range (LR) interactions, leading current approaches to rely on explicit physics-based terms or components. In this work, we propose AllScAIP, a straightforward, attention-based, and energy-conserving MLIP model that scales to O(100 million) training samples. It addresses the long-range challenge using an all-to-all node attention component that is data-driven. Extensive ablations reveal that in low-data/small-model regimes, inductive biases improve sample efficiency. However, as data and model size scale, these benefits diminish or even reverse, while all-to-all attention remains critical for capturing LR interactions. Our model achieves state-of-the-art energy/force accuracy on molecular systems, as well as a number of physics-based evaluations (OMol25), while being competitive on materials (OMat24) and catalysts (OC20). Furthermore, it enables stable, long-timescale MD simulations that accurately recover experimental observables, including density and heat of vaporization predictions.

AI.tldr: AllScAIP is a scalable attention-based MLIP that uses all-to-all node attention to capture long-range interactions, achieving SOTA accuracy on molecular systems while enabling stable MD simulations.
AI.motivation: Current MLIPs struggle with long-range interactions in large systems like biomolecules and electrolytes, forcing reliance on explicit physics-based terms that may limit scalability and accuracy.
AI.method: Proposes AllScAIP - an attention-based, energy-conserving MLIP with all-to-all node attention component that is data-driven, scaling to O(100 million) training samples without explicit physics-based long-range terms.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art energy/force accuracy on molecular systems (OMol25), competitive performance on materials (OMat24) and catalysts (OC20), and enables stable long-timescale MD simulations that recover experimental observables like density and heat of vaporization.
AI.conclusion: As data and model size scale, inductive biases become less beneficial while all-to-all attention remains critical for capturing long-range interactions, suggesting a shift toward more data-driven approaches in large-scale MLIP development.

title: HiPP-Prune: Hierarchical Preference-Conditioned Structured Pruning for Vision-Language Models
summary: Pruning vision-language models (VLMs) for efficient deployment is challenging because compression can affect not only task utility but also visual grounding, often amplifying object hallucinations even at the same sparsity level. We present HiPP-Prune, a hierarchical preference-conditioned structured pruning framework that treats pruning as conditional resource allocation under multiple objectives. HiPP-Prune makes plan-level decisions: a single policy invocation outputs a global pruning blueprint by factorizing decisions into an overall sparsity budget and a layer-wise allocation, enabling queryable trade-offs via a user-specified preference vector. To account for VLM-specific failure modes, our policy state integrates a visual sensitivity signal derived from attention flow between vision tokens and language hidden states, discouraging over-pruning of vision-critical layers that facilitate cross-modal fusion. We optimize pruning plans with plan-level Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) under a multi-objective return that combines task utility, hallucination robustness (POPE), compression, and a synaptic-flow-inspired stability proxy to reduce unproductive exploration in high-sparsity regimes. Experiments on LLaVA with POPE and ScienceQA demonstrate that HiPP-Prune discovers diverse non-dominated pruning plans and provides controllable robustness--utility trade-offs under matched sparsity budgets.

AI.tldr: HiPP-Prune is a hierarchical preference-conditioned pruning framework for vision-language models that treats pruning as conditional resource allocation, enabling controllable trade-offs between task utility, hallucination robustness, and compression.
AI.motivation: Pruning VLMs is challenging because compression affects both task utility and visual grounding, often amplifying object hallucinations even at the same sparsity level. Current methods don't adequately address VLM-specific failure modes like hallucination amplification.
AI.method: Hierarchical preference-conditioned structured pruning framework that makes plan-level decisions: a single policy outputs global pruning blueprint by factorizing decisions into overall sparsity budget and layer-wise allocation. Integrates visual sensitivity signal from attention flow between vision tokens and language hidden states. Optimizes with plan-level Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) under multi-objective return combining task utility, hallucination robustness (POPE), compression, and synaptic-flow-inspired stability proxy.
AI.result: Experiments on LLaVA with POPE and ScienceQA show that HiPP-Prune discovers diverse non-dominated pruning plans and provides controllable robustness-utility trade-offs under matched sparsity budgets.
AI.conclusion: HiPP-Prune effectively addresses VLM-specific pruning challenges by enabling preference-conditioned resource allocation that balances multiple objectives including hallucination mitigation, offering a framework for controllable compression of vision-language models.

title: Learning to Generate via Understanding: Understanding-Driven Intrinsic Rewarding for Unified Multimodal Models
summary: Recently, unified multimodal models (UMMs) have made remarkable progress in integrating visual understanding and generation, demonstrating strong potential for complex text-to-image (T2I) tasks. Despite their theoretical promise, a persistent capability gap exists: UMMs typically exhibit superior visual understanding but comparatively weaker generative capabilities. This discrepancy arises largely from the intrinsic decoupling between the understanding and generation processes. While a UMM can accurately interpret fine-grained visual details, it often struggles to produce semantically coherent images from complex textual prompts. To address this challenge, we explore UMMs' internal understanding capability to enhance generation quality. We propose a token-level intrinsic text-image alignment reward mechanism, GvU, enabling the UMM to act simultaneously as teacher and student: it evaluates its own outputs using the understanding branch to guide the generations accordingly. Building upon this, we design a self-supervised reinforcement learning framework, allowing UMMs to iteratively improve their generation quality through understanding-based intrinsic reward signals--without reliance on external supervision. Experimental results show that our method substantially boosts UMMs' generation, which in turn strengthens their fine-grained visual understanding, narrowing the capability gap between UMMs' visual understanding and generation.

AI.tldr: UMMs have strong visual understanding but weak generation. Proposed GvU uses understanding branch as intrinsic reward to guide generation via self-supervised RL, closing the capability gap.
AI.motivation: Unified multimodal models show promise but have a persistent capability gap: strong visual understanding but comparatively weak generative capabilities. This decoupling between understanding and generation processes limits their effectiveness in complex text-to-image tasks.
AI.method: Proposed GvU - a token-level intrinsic text-image alignment reward mechanism that enables UMMs to act as both teacher and student. Uses understanding branch to evaluate outputs and guide generation. Built a self-supervised reinforcement learning framework for iterative improvement without external supervision.
AI.result: Method substantially boosts UMMs' generation capabilities, which in turn strengthens their fine-grained visual understanding. Successfully narrows the capability gap between visual understanding and generation in unified multimodal models.
AI.conclusion: Leveraging UMMs' internal understanding capability through intrinsic reward mechanisms and self-supervised RL effectively bridges the understanding-generation gap, enhancing both capabilities simultaneously without external supervision.

title: GenHOI: Towards Object-Consistent Hand-Object Interaction with Temporally Balanced and Spatially Selective Object Injection
summary: Hand-Object Interaction (HOI) remains a core challenge in digital human video synthesis, where models must generate physically plausible contact and preserve object identity across frames. Although recent HOI reenactment approaches have achieved progress, they are typically trained and evaluated in-domain and fail to generalize to complex, in-the-wild scenarios. In contrast, all-in-one video editing models exhibit broader robustness but still struggle with HOI-specific issues such as inconsistent object appearance. In this paper, we present GenHOI, a lightweight augmentation to pretrained video generation models that injects reference-object information in a temporally balanced and spatially selective manner.
